SECTION 9.3 - Magic Items
-------------------------

Standard magical melee weapons and thrown weapons add their magical bonus both
both to hit and to damage.  Bows and crossbows also do the same (they only add
to hit starting in Secret of the Silver Blades); arrows and bolts add their
magical bonus only to damage.

Standard magical armor add their plus as a bonus to Armor Class when worn, and
if its base armor type decreases movement, the magical version increases net
movement by 3.

Magical scrolls come in both clerical and magic-user types.    Spellcasting
from a scroll is instantaneous, and have an effective spell level of whichever
is higher of either 6, or the minimum class level needed to cast the spell plus
1.  (So in Pool of Radiance, it will always be 6.)

Magic-user spells may be permanently learned from the scroll, which consumes
that use of the spell.  In later games, thieves of at least level 10 may
attempt to use such scrolls.

Clerical spells include Restoration, which in this game may only be cast from
scrolls.  This will restore lost levels to a character that has been drained by
level-draining undead, but you will still lose any progress towards the next
level.

Magical wands start with a defined number of charges if not random, often less
than the 80+1d20 you would find in the tabletop game.  In general, charged
items in the tabletop game are far more useful than they are in CRPGs; regular
spellcasting is much faster and stronger than it is supposed to be.

If using a magical item that is not a scroll, it has an effective spell level
of 12.

You may not equip more than two magical rings at once.

Other magical items include:

Bag of Holding -- If equipped, causes all coins and unequipped items to have no
  weight, with an upper limit of 5000 coins of extra carrying capacity.  It may
  take multiple pick up attempts to actually pick up this extra weight (which
  is a bug).  (This is equivalent to the type the DMG says you get on a roll of
  31...70.)
Bracers of Defense ("Bracers AC #") -- These do not count as armor and will not
  stack with it, but will set base Armor Class to the indicated number.  It's
  generally the defensive equipment of choice on thieves and pure magic-users.
Cloak of Displacement -- Grants a -2 bonus to Armor Class; the first physical
  attack per combat will automatically miss.  Also grants a +2 bonus to saving
  throws if found as a random item; the one guarded by the basilisk in Mendor's
  Library doesn't.  (If Dispel Magic is cast upon the wearer, you may need to
  unequip and re-equip the cloak for it to work again.)
Cloak of Elvenkind -- This makes the wearer invisible 95% of the time; this is
  checked every minute, and if the roll succeeds the wearer is granted
  invisibility for 1 minute.  (In the tabletop version, you must be outside,
  and the quality of the invisibility depends upon terrain.)
Cursed Necklace -- This penalizes both AC and all saving throws by 5.  This is
  bad for actual gameplay, but fantastic for experiments.
Dust of Disappearance -- This will make invisible:
  * out of combat: the entire party for (2d10) * 10 minutes
  * in combat: up to 4 creatures at range 1 for (1d10 + 10) * 10 minutes
  This is a special form of invisibility that is not dispelled by attacking or
  spellcasting, nor does the ability to see invisible work on it.  Yes, it is
  very powerful -- there is a reason this does not exist at all in Gold Box
  games later than Curse of the Azure Bonds.  This effect is described in
  "D"isplay as "invisible", which differs from the normal "invisibility".
Efreeti Bottle -- The efreeti in this bottle will emerge to help you fight
  against the vampire in the Valhingen Graveyard when you first encounter it.
Gauntlets of Ogre Power -- Wearing this raises Strength to 18(00).
Javelin of Lightning -- This single-shot consumable creates a lightning bolt
  that does 20+1d6 electrical damage.  This acts like the magic-user spell, but
  with a range of 9, and the bolt only travels 3 squares.
Keoghtum's Ointment -- A jar of this has 5 applications, each of which will
  restores 8+1d4 hit points of damage and cure disease.  (The tabletop version
  of "Keoghtom's" ointment also neutralizes poison.  The Gold Box version seems
  to convert poisoned characters to outright dead ones.  I don't think that's
  the intended meaning of "neutralize".)
Long Sword +2 Flame Tongue -- This is supposed to do extra damage against
  creatures that use cold, birds, undead, or fire-vulnerable creatures, but it
  doesn't.  It also should prevent trolls from regenerating, but that doesn't
  happen either (and it doesn't do extra damage against those as it should).
  So this is just a Long Sword +2.
Manual of Bodily Health -- Using this book consumes it; after 24 hours, the
  user will start to train over the course of one month (always 30 days in the
  Forgotten Realms).  At the end, +1 permanent Constitution is gained.  An
  attempt to adjust hit points is made, but that function has several bugs.
  Unlike the tabletop game, you may benefit from such an item more than once,
  and you can in fact use and benefit from several at once.  This is the only
  way to permanently gain base statistics in any of the Gold Box games (without
  exploiting transfer bugs).
Necklace of Missiles -- shoots range 7 fireballs (as the 3rd-level magic-user
  spell) that do (1d3 * 2 + 1)d6 damage.  Normally, this only has several
  charges; if you find this as a random magical item, it typically has 20.
  (This is closest to the type rolled on 9...12 in the DMG.)
Potion of Extra Healing -- has three doses, each of which restores 1d8 hit
  points of damage; internally identical to casting Cure Light Wounds.
Potion of Giant Strength -- grants frost giant (21) Strength for 4+1d4 turns
  (50-80 minutes), which confers the ability to throw boulders.
Potion of Healing -- restores 2d4+2 hit points of damage.
Potion of Speed -- acts as a Haste spell that lasts for 5d4 rounds: removes
  any Slow effect, and doubles movement and number of physical attacks, at the
  cost of magically aging the user by 1 year.
Ring of Feather Falling -- prevents falling damage.  Or at least it is supposed
  to; it doesn't actually prevent damage from falls that I have seen.
Ring of Fire Resistance -- grants a +4 bonus to saving throws against fire, and
  subtracts 2 (minimum 1) from each die of damage.
Ring of Invisibility -- this casts the Invisibility spell on the wearer when
  equipped, at the end of every round of combat.  This is unlimited, but the
  invisibility effect lasts only for 1 minute every time.
Ring of Protection -- this grants its bonus of the best ring: (1) to Armor
  Class if the wearer is not also wearing magical armor; (2) to all saving
  throws.  The second effect is not present on most Rings of Protection; the
  only one I know of where this actually works is the one carried by the
  THEURGIST (#110).  (The saving throw bonus becomes more common in later
  Gold Box games.)
Sling of Seeking +2 -- this grants its bonus both to hit and to damage.
Wand of Fireballs -- shoots fireballs (as the 3rd-level magic-user spell) which
  which do 6d6 damage.  (The saving throw should be vs. rod/staff/wand, but is
  vs. spell.)
Wand of Lightning (Bolt) -- shoots lightning bolts (as the 3rd-level magic-user
  spell) which do 6d6 damage.  (The saving throw should be vs. rod/staff/wand,
  but is vs. spell.)
Wand of Magic Missiles -- shoots 2 magic missiles (as the 1st-level magic-user
  spell), which do 2d4+2 damage at range 12 to a single target (and aren't
  blocked by the Shield spell).
Wand of Paralyzation -- attempts to hold any single monster (range 6) for 5d4
  rounds; a saving throw vs. paralyzation negates.  (The DMG says to use rod/
  staff/wand.)

Magical treasure is also worth experience.  Any magical item with a plus is
worth 400 XP per plus.  Other cases:
* Bracers or Cloaks of Displacement count as +1 per point of AC they grant;
* Minuses do not count against you;
* A stack of ammunition counts as a single item;
* Scrolls count usually count as +1 (always if randomly generated), sometimes
  +2;
* Miscellaneous magical items usually count as +1 (always if randomly
  generated), but can be higher (some placed wands count as +10).

SECTION 9.4 - Random Magical Items
----------------------------------

Here's how random magical generation works in v1.3 (it's known to work
differently in v1.0, or in any other Gold Box game):

Roll 1d100 for item type:

* Item type 1...60: weapon/armor.  Roll 1d100 for weapon/armor type, then roll
  1d20 for plus: 1...14 is +1, 15...19 is +2, 20 is +3.
  * Weapon/armor type 1...20, 22...44, 46...47, or 50...59: weapon or armor of
    this specific type (see section 9.2 for which one).  Darts are generated
    in stacks of 5, bolts in stacks of 10, anything else is singular.
  * Weapon/armor type 21: javelin.  Roll 1d5 for javelin type:
    * Javelin type 1...4: magical regular Javelin
    * Javelin type 5: should be a Javelin of Lightning, but it looks like there
      was a typo in the source here; you'll instead get a buggy item with
      hard-to-predict name and behavior.
  * Weapon/armor type 45: this normally would be a Fine Composite Long Bow, but
    this is special-cased so you can't actually get a magical one; this is
    replaced by a Shield (which is also possible in the generic list).
  * Weapon/armor type 60...90: sword.  Roll 1d10 (all of these are also in
    the above generic list):
    * Sword type 1...4: Long Sword
    * Sword type 5...7: Broad Sword
    * Sword type 8: Bastard Sword
    * Sword type 9: Short Sword
    * Sword type 10: Two-Handed Sword
  * Weapon/armor type 91...94: Arrows, in a stack of 10
  * Weapon/armor type 95...97: Ring of Protection.  Only provides an AC bonus
    and not also a saving throw bonus.
  * Weapon/armor type 98...100: Bracers of Defense.  The magical plus is
    converted here to AC 6 (70%), AC 4 (25%), or AC 2 (5%).
  * Weapon/armor type 48...49: Not handled in the code; this results in an item
    with no name that does nothing.
* Item type 61...85: Magic-user scroll.  Roll 1d3 for number of spells, then
  for each spell roll 1d3 for spell level.  Spells of the same level have equal
  probability.  Always worth 400 XP.
* Item type 86...92: Clerical scroll.  Roll 1d3 for number of spells, the
  for each spell roll 1d3 for spell level; Restoration is not possible.  Spells
  of the same level have equal probability.  Always worth 400 XP.
* Item type 93...98: (technically 91...98, but 91...92 are trapped by the
  above) Miscellaneous item.  Except as noted, any item generated either here
  or in the 99...100 roll is worth 400 XP.
  * Miscellaneous item type 1...7: Non-Giant-Strength potion.  Roll 1d11 for
    type:
    * Potion type 1...5: Potion of Healing
    * Potion type 6...8: Potion of Extra Healing
    * Potion type 9: Potion of Speed
    * Potion type 10: Keoghtum's Ointment (5 applications), only possible here
    * Potion type 11: buggy item with hard-to-predict name and behavior
  * Miscellaneous item type 8...9: Other item.  Roll 1d10 for type:
    * Other item type 1...7: Necklace of Missiles (20 charges)
    * Other item type 8...10: Bag of Holding, only possible here
  * Miscellaneous item type 10: Potion of Giant Strength
  * Miscellaneous item type 11...12: Non-Magic-Missile wand.  Roll 1d3+1 for
    type:
    * Wand type 1: Wand of Lightning (can't get here: can't roll 1 on 1d3+1)
    * Wand type 2: Wand of Paralyzation (20 charges)
    * Wand type 3: Wand of Fireballs (20 charges), only possible here
    * Wand type 4: buggy item with hard-to-predict name and behavior
  * Miscellaneous item type 13...14: Wand of Magic Missiles (30 charges)
  * Miscellaneous item type 15: Cloak of Displacement, worth 800 XP; also
    provides a +2 bonus to all saving throws, unlike non-random Cloaks of
    Displacement.
  * Miscellaneous item type 16: Cloak of Elvenkind, only possible here
* Type type 99...100: Non-Protection ring.  Roll 1d10 for type:
  * Ring type 1: Ring of Feather Falling
  * Ring type 2...6: Ring of Fire Resistance
  * Ring type 7...10: Ring of Invisibility, only possible here

SECTION 9.5 - Summary of Locations with Magic Items
---------------------------------------------------

Here is a checklist of noteworthy magical items you may find in the game, by
location.  Numbers indicate locations on the corresponding maps.  This is not
comprehensive for sake of brevity; check the maps' notes for more details.

Civilized Area (New Phlan)
Once you become strong enough, and are about to be rewarded for completing a
commission (and you don't mind being a murderhobo), you can fight the city
guards for magical items, then get the mission reward to negate anger:
4TH LVL FIGHTER (#41): Long Sword +1, Chain Mail +1
6TH LVL FIGHTER (#75): Two-Handed Sword +2, Chain Mail +1, Javelin of Lightning
7TH LVL CLERIC (#83): Mace +2, Banded Mail +2
8TH LVL FIGHTER (#85): Two-Handed Sword +2, Plate Mail +2, Ring of Protection
  +3
LEVEL 3 MU (#94): Wand of Magic Missiles (67 charges)

The Slums
1 - Clerical Scroll (Cure Light Wounds, Cure Light Wounds)
2 - Leather Armor +1
3 - 1 random magic item

6 - Bracers AC 6
9 - 3 Broad Swords +1, Chain Mail +1, Flail +1
10 - Ring of Protection +1, Short Bow +1
12 - Short Bow +1, 20 Arrows +1
16 - Shield +1, Magic-User Scroll (Magic Missile)
20 - 3 random magic items

Sokal Keep
15 - Hammer +1
17 - Shield +1, Long Sword +1, Chain Mail +1, Mace +2

Kuto's Well
22 - Banded Mail +1, Quarter Staff +1, Bracers AC 4

    Clerical Scroll (Restoration, Restoration)

Cadorna Textile House

     Magic-User Scroll (Burning Hands, Mirror Image, Blink)

     to exploit a bug to get this more than once)

Kovel Mansion

     Magic-User Scroll (Blink, Ray of Enfeeblement, Slow),
     Clerical Scroll (Hold Person, Cure Blindness, Bestow Curse),
     Clerical Scroll (Prayer, Remove Curse, Animate Dead),
     Magic-User Scroll (Reduce, Detect Invisibility, Fireball),
     Magic-User Scroll (Burning Hands, Mirror Image, Blink)

    Clerical Scroll (Animate Dead, Cure Blindness)

    Magic-User Scroll (Fireball, Ray of Enfeeblement, Stinking Cloud)

     charges), Dust of Disappearance

Wilderness Lairs (all repeatable)
Western Wilderness, Thri-Kreen Lair 13: 1 random magic item
Eastern Wilderness, Wyvern Lair 2: 1 random magic item
Eastern Wilderness, Giant Lizard Lair 8: 1 random magic item

Nomad Camp
If you attack the nomads: Long Sword +2, Shield +1, Scale Mail +2, Dagger +2,
  Bracers AC 4 (says AC 2, actually is AC 4), Wand of Magic Missiles (50
  charges)
If you attack only the kobolds: Two-Handed Sword +2, Wand of Magic Missiles
  (35 charges)

Kobold Caves
9 - 2 Two-Handed Swords +2
26 - 2 random magic items

     Magic-User Scroll (Friends, Invisibility, Protection from Normal Missiles)

Yarash's Pyramid

Lizardman Keep
P4, P5, P6 (three different locations) - Shield +2

Buccaneer Base
The captain carries: Long Sword +4, Shield +2, Plate Mail +3

Zhentil Keep Outpost
5 - Gauntlets of Ogre Power, Plate Mail +2, Shield +1, 2 Rings of Fire
    Resistance, 3 Potions of Extra Healing, Javelin of Lightning, Bracers AC 3,
    Wand of Lightning (20 charges)

    Restoration)

     Clerical Scroll (Restoration, Restoration),
     Magic-User Scroll (Slow, Hold Person, Invisibility 10' Radius)

     Shield +1, 4 Clerical Scrolls (Restoration, Restoration)

     5 Clerical Scrolls (Restoration, Restoration)

SECTION 10.1 - Notes on Creature Statistics
-------------------------------------------

Many monsters capable of using equipment can equip or remove weapons, shields,
and sometimes even armor on the fly.  Their actual statistics in combat may not
reflect the details in the next section, especially if you surprise them before
they can ready equipment.  It is assumed that enemies with both melee and
ranged weaponry are using the former.

THAC0, damage and AC are adjusted for monsters according to equipment; these
statistics (for melee) are shown below.  THAC0 is not recalculated for
Strength or Dexterity.  Armor supersedes base AC, but only if it is better.

No other attributes are adjusted; a humanoid with a Ring of Fire Resistance
does not actually resist fire, movement is not affected by armor, and fighter-
types do not get number of attacks recalculated.

If a creature has secondary attacks, those are used before primary attacks.

A "Larger or larger" monster may be so in two ways: by being declared that way,
or by having an icon of more than one tile.  Either way, large monster weapon
damage applies to it.
* The note (small icon) indicates it has a one-tile icon.
* The note (reversed) indicates it is declared small/medium in the game files.

Hit dice are important to many spell effects, though the Gold Box games do not
bother to track partial hit dice as the tabletop game does.  Every 4 fractional
hit dice are generally converted to a single whole hit die.

Non-character base statistics are used as follows:
  Strength - affects melee weapon damage (but not unarmed attacks)
  Intelligence - affects decision to flee or surrender
  Wisdom - no effect
  Dexterity - affects initiative and AC
  Constitution - no effect
  Charisma - no effect

Monster #82 never appears anywhere.

SECTION 10.2 - Detailed Bestiary
--------------------------------

Hit Points:  55  Movement:  24   Size: Large or larger
Strength     19      Base saving throws versus:
Intelligence 10      Paralyzation, Poison, or Death  8
Wisdom       10      Rod, Staff, or Wand             9
Dexterity    10      Petrification or Polymorph      9
Constitution 10      Breath Weapon                   9
Charisma     10      Spell                          10
THAC0:  10  Armor Class: 2 (from behind: 4)
Physical attack: 1 for 3d8 damage
Special Abilities: #113 efreeti fire resistance (-1 damage per die of fire
  damage)
Money carried: 6 gold
Experience for defeating: 1950 + 14 per hit point (2720) + 6 for money (2726)
Differences from tabletop: lacks Enlarge and Invisibility spells


SECTION 11.1 - Cheat Mode
-------------------------

To begin the game in cheat mode, start the game with using the executable
option STING, which must be all capitalized.  That means invoking the game with
"start STING" (or "st STING" if you have that version), or editing any batch
file to say that.

This activates the following keys:
Ctrl-C: immediately quit to DOS (this command does exist in later games)
Ctrl-D: toggle hit point debugging (displays debugging upon a level drain)
Alt-X: immediately win a combat
J: (in a training hall) train for free

It also completely bypasses both the introduction and the copy protection.

SECTION 11.2 - Raising the Dead with Targeted Damage Spells
-----------------------------------------------------------

A dead character is a valid target in combat for a targeted spell that can
inflict damage.  A dead character has 0 hit points, and if the spell does fewer
than 10 points of damage, the dead character will now just be dying, and can
then be bandaged and cured as normal.

Burning Hands always works for this in Pool of Radiance.  Cause Light Wounds
can also work, but requires an attack roll to work.

This bug is still present in Curse of the Azure Bonds, but gone in Secret of
the Silver Blades.

SECTION 11.3 - Duplicating Items
--------------------------------

This interface loophole allows you to duplicate any item you own without using
a save game editor.  To do so, perform the following steps (back up your saved
game directory before doing this; it is easy to screw up):

   1. Go to a training hall.  (In later games you can do this outside a
      training hall.)
   2. "R"emove one character from the party.
   3. "S"ave the game.
   4. Create a dummy character.
   5. Trade all the items to be duplicated to the dummy character.
   6. "R"emove the dummy character from the party.
   7. "D"rop all other characters.
   8. "L"oad the saved game from step 3 or 11b.
   9. "A"dd the dummy character to the party.
  10. You should now have two copies of the desired items; transfer them to
      your real characters.
  11. If you want more copies:
      a. "R"emove the dummy character from the party.
      b. "S"ave the game.
      c. "A"dd the dummy character back to the party.
      d. Go back to step 5.
  12. Once done, discard the dummy character and re-add the removed real
      character.

SECTION 11.4 - Abusing Abnormal Constitution
--------------------------------------------

You can abuse out-of-range Constituion, with a score of either 22 or 0, which
you can get by:
* Finding the Manual of Bodily Health in Mendor's Library, and get many copies
  by restarting the game repeatedly, or duplicating it.  Use them one at a time
  to raise Constitution to exactly 22.
* Draining a character to Constitution 0 by repeatedly dying and having a
  temple cast Raise Dead.

You then get an abnormally high number of hit points per level: +7 or +5 at
Constitution 22, or +8 at Constitution 0.  You can "M"odify a character with 0
XP for more hit points if you really want to get the most out of this.

Other abnormal Constitution scores either will not work or have undesirable
effects.

You do not get to keep a Constitution of 22 in later games (if you directly
transfer to Curse of the Azure Bonds), but you do keep any hit points (unless
you transfer to Hillsfar).  If you abuse a Constitution of 0, that will not
behave well in later games; raise it before leaving Pool of Radiance.  Be aware
of the limit of 255 hit points -- that always exists even in later games.


SECTION 12.1.1 - Useful Hacking Utilities and References
--------------------------------------------------------

Gold Box Companion is not just useful for playing the game; it now includes
numerous utilities useful for hacking.
http://gbc.zorbus.net/

DAXDump and ECLDump can be used to unpack the *.DAX files.  You may download
it from:
http://gbc.zorbus.net/daxdump.zip

Gold Box Explorer will allow you to investigate the contests of *.DAX and
*.ECL files.  Its project page is at:
https://github.com/bsimser/Gold-Box-Explorer
The Curse of the Azure Bonds remake is for the sequel, but it was helpful for
figuring out Gold Box-specific mechanics:
http://simeonpilgrim.com/blog/2008/07/22/curse-of-the-azure-bonds-build-1013/

The executable is partially compressed.  You can use UNP to decompress it
(which should not interfere with playing the game), site requires using the
Internet Archive:
http://unp.bencastricum.nl/

To disassemble the executables, IDA Pro v5 Free works well:
https://downloads.scummvm.org/frs/extras/IDA/idafree50.exe
Ghidra might be the superior tool someday, but among other problems it cannot
natively handle DOS overlays, which is a deal-killer for the Gold Box games.
IDA Pro v6.* works significantly better, if you can get your hands on it; the
manufacturer no longer sells or supports it, and v7+ won't work for DOS
analysis.  IDA Pro v6.* adds support for pointers in structs and improves
__usercall but still does not truly understand Turbo Pascal, and you still
can't do some things like define an array of function pointers, or specify
a parameter in 3 separate registers (both of which happen in the Gold Box
games).

x86 assembly reference:
https://c9x.me/x86/

x86 interrupt reference:
https://www.cs.cmu.edu/~ralf/files.html

DOS memory map:
https://www.osdata.com/system/physical/memmap.htm
https://www.osdata.com/system/physical/lowmem.htm

TPv6PG refers to the Turbo Pascal v6 Programmer's Guide.
TPv6LR refers to the Turbo Pascal v6 Library Reference.

SECTION 12.1.2 - Program Overview
---------------------------------

Pool of Radiance is written in Pascal.  One way to eliminate most possible
languages is to inspect human-readable strings in the executable related to
normal program function: standard Pascal strings begin with the length of the
string, but C strings do not and are always null-terminated.  (You cannot use
fatal error messages or DOS interrupt calls for this purpose.  This assumes the
strings were placed by the compiler -- all bets are off if a programmer did so
manually, which does happen here sometimes.)

SSI used Turbo Pascal for the Gold Box games -- you can confirm by comparing
library functions written in machine code with the Turbo Pascal compiler.  Pool
of Radiance v1.0 must have been written in Turbo Pascal v4; v3 doesn't support
unsigned 16-bit integers and produces rather bloated COM files instead of EXE
files (COM is normally what you want for a slim executable, but not in this
case), and v5 didn't yet exist.  Pool of Radiance v1.3 was compiled with the
later version of Turbo Pascal v6; there were two different releases of v6, and
the one SSI used is dated 1991.  (If you thought present-day versioning is
stupid ... the 1980s and 1990s were much worse.)

This long ago, games were typically written in whatever language the
programmers felt like; for instance, in 1980s DOS games SSI used:
* Gold Box games (before Dark Queen of Krynn): Turbo Pascal v4 to v6
* Phantasie I: non-Microsoft C of some kind
* Rings of Zilfin: Turbo Pascal v3
* Shard of Spring: Microsoft QuickBasic v3
* Star Command: Microsoft QuickBasic v2

Borland did not always put their own company name in every executable its
compilers produced; Pool of Radiance v1.3 does say Borland, but anything
compiled with Turbo Pascal v4/v5 will not unless the programmers make it so.
(Microsoft is more reliable about stamping their company name on everything.)

Quick reference on Turbo Pascal data types:
Array:
  * Indexing can work however a programmer pleases, so long as it is 16-bit;
    0-indexing and 1-indexing are both common; 2-indexing, 3-indexing, and
    9-indexing all exist here as well.
  * Multi-dimensional arrays are possible, but ordinarily must be rectangular;
    any "ragged" multi-dimensional array of the sort used in C++ or most modern
    programming languages would have to be micromanaged here.
  * Multi-dimensional arrays are row-major, same as C/C++.
  * An array does not undergo automatic decay to a pointer as in C; that must
    be specified by a programmer.
Boolean:
  * 0 is False, 1 is True.  Taken for granted today, but this standard wasn't
    standard back then; -1 for True was more common then than now.
  * Takes 1 byte, which may not be the case in other languages.
Byte: unsigned 8-bit integer (uint8).  (Not to be confused with a byte.)
Char: 8-bit character.
  * Can be either a Byte or a String of length 1.
  * It is assumed your code page (normally 437 in English, or 850 for other
    Western European languages) is a superset of standard ASCII, and that
    character 219 is a solid block.
  * Note that an 8-bit Byte is unsigned; a char is not necessarily unsigned in
    other programming languages.
dictionary or HashMap equivalent: Turbo Pascal never had this.
File: represents a DOS file handle, with extra information.
  * May be either text or not.
  * For File structure, see TPv6PG, p. 143.  Any strings in a File are C
    strings, not Pascal Strings.
  * A File takes 256 bytes if opened as text, 128 otherwise.
  * May never be passed by value; this is the only type in Turbo Pascal that
    must be passed by reference.
floating-point other than Real (Single, Double, Extended): Not used by Pool of
  Radiance.
Integer: signed 16-bit integer (int16).
Longint: signed 32-bit integer (int32).  There is no way to express your own
  unsigned 32-bit integer (uint32) in Turbo Pascal v4/v5/v6 without resorting
  to inline machine code or floating-point.
Object: C++ object equivalent.  SSI can't have used Objects; they didn't exist
  until Turbo Pascal v5.5.  v6 actually has much of the structure many modern
  programmers expect.
Pointer: pointers accessible to a Turbo Pascal programmer are always far
  pointers: 32-bit, with 21 bits of actual information.  Internally used
  pointers may be near pointers.
Real: 48-bit floating point; 8-bit exponent, 40-bit little-endian mantissa with
  most significant bit the sign.  Doesn't adhere to IEEE 754, as Real predates
  it.  48 bits is the largest an Intel 8088 can handle two of using only
  registers.  Actually used by the Gold Box games, which is not what most
  programmers even from the 1990s would expect.
Record: C struct equivalent.  The Turbo Pascal compiler is not allowed to
  reorder Record fields, same as C/C++ compilers.  In x86/x64 architecture,
  this helps serve as a simple serialization and deserialization mechanism, if
  you declare your Record/struct as packed; SSI took advantage of this.
Set: bitset with maximum of 256 bits; often occupies the maximum 32 bytes.
  Implemented with brute force, not hash tables (brute force is much faster at
  this small scale, especially on old hardware).
Shortint: signed 8-bit integer (int8).  (A C/C++ short is 16-bit or more.)
String:
  * First byte is string length, followed by string data; maximum length 255.
  * No null terminator is required.
  * Thus, chars are 1-indexed, not 0-indexed as in C/C++.
  * A stack variable that occupies precisely 256 bytes is typically a String.
    In the Gold Box games, this also applies to variables that take precisely
    41 bytes, as the screen width is 40 char.
  * Pascal strings are faster to handle than C strings, as you don't incur the
    cost of checking every byte to find the end of the string, but are
    otherwise more awkward for many many reasons.
  * Another major problem: Turbo Pascal by default passes strings by value,
    which means lots of code and time are occupied by repeated String copying.
  * Additionally awkward: SSI implements multiple additional kinds of strings,
    including C strings.  C string support was only added in Turbo Pascal v7;
    SSI had to roll their own C string code.
Word: unsigned 16-bit integer (uint16).

All numbers and addresses are little-endian unless otherwise specified;
exception: multi-register expressions (e.g., DX:AX) are big-endian.

Bytes are 8-bit unless otherwise specified.  Yes, this is one of those cases
where bytes are not necessarily 8-bit.  That's not unusual in games originally
written for 8-bit platforms then ported to a 16-bit platform.

The default convention is that integers are unsigned.  This is actually what
you generally want for a game on an 8-bit or 16-bit platform, especially in
Turbo Pascal.  Unlike Infocom or Microprose, SSI often adheres to that unless
it thinks it has good reason not to.  (I wouldn't do this for a non-game, or on
a 32-bit platform.)

The default base convention I use:
  Hexadecimal:
    1. addresses
       a. segments
       b. offsets
       c. GBVM addresses (addresses begin with $ if and only if they are GBVM)
    2. opcodes
    3. machine code bytes
  Decimal: everything else (even bit flags)

One other point that should be emphasized about Turbo Pascal: it compiles very
quickly and works more reliably compared to C, but as a trade-off is prone to
producing machine code that is bloated and sluggish; the continual copying of
Strings all over the place is the most prominent example of this.  That makes
Pascal a superior learning language and makes life easier as a programmer, but
if performance matters (which it does here, even with emulation on a modern
computer), you're essentially making life easier for yourself but harder for
the user.  If you programmed back in the day and wondered why teaching was done
in Pascal but actual coding was done in C, you now have some idea why.

Another point that I feel compelled to mention: most of what I've written
anywhere in this FAQ is the result of reverse engineering (roughly comparable
to what most biologists do -- that's my actual professional training).  It
works rather differently from the forward engineering that almost all software
engineers do.  The generally reasonable assumptions that software engineers
make often do *not* apply in reverse.

SECTION 12.1.3 - File Manifest
------------------------------

This is a list of files included with installed original v1.2 and v1.3.

* v1.0 and v1.1 are not listed because (1) I don't have original copies of
  those versions; (2) there are substantial differences between v1.1 and v1.2.
* Anything not listed is not necessary for gameplay; the main omissions are
  POOL.CFG (which varies with the settings you choose) and the default party.
* If no version difference is listed, the file is unchanged between v1.2 and
  v1.3.
* Timestamps are not listed.  It can be useful as a makeshift means of file ID,
  and occasionally an older game will use it for this purpose; but here as in
  many other such cases, the installation executable may set some timestamps
  itself, and the GOG release does not preserve it.  (Size still works as
  makeshift file ID; that's similar to using birthdate as a makeshift personal
  ID.)
* The given SHA-1 hash is an actual good means of file ID.
