SPRIT8   DAX   14,853 3038b3a808055a186b441b9187b1f61934cf7672
SQRPACI  DAX   69,788 3351c1eebf0352663bc053a2596eb92c1c88df9b
START    EXE   75,536 bd814a271139ca080c2b2e9d6418f381265f4da7 (v1.2)
               65,231 0640ba4fabdc9120dc89c518fab04e388164e2a2 (v1.3)
TITLE    DAX   33,898 39170f1280b6332e69695fbd8390554c339d1da0
WALLDEF1 DAX    2,147 34dd7128cffc194293ffd70ddcef94c97a58997e
WALLDEF2 DAX    4,044 46fa9788dffc6a1a9a042326da4226a2bb0d1663
WALLDEF3 DAX    4,289 51f750491762fc8264d750e91911855a905524ce
WALLDEF4 DAX    4,956 23a11eaa74da8cfbae2096bf008cfc740970568e
WALLDEF5 DAX    2,121 37bb9622c48e38fafdd6fee60df281e444bc0fc8
WALLDEF6 DAX    3,492 c13682fe7be1d307bf42a796b70d4e1c6a9b9ef1
WALLDEF7 DAX    3,176 09aca6c662c47ff3d3e7311a059e1e018e4edc9f
WALLDEF8 DAX    4,998 21e14240f82dacc0b2d56649e74f03e23563f7f9
WILDCOM  DAX    5,786 f17701ad0612056574e300cd22acd8aec551745c

START.EXE (ST.EXE in some distributions; there is no functional difference) and
GAME.OVR are the compiled executable.  This is usually one file for either
small programs or modern ones; this split, common in DOS, is roughly
analogous to living organism's genome being split over multiple chromosomes.

START.EXE and GAME.OVR have the following original timestamps:
* v1.2: 1989-05-09 14:30
* v1.3: 1992-09-04 03:17

For other files, any digit refers to the disk number.  This is the actual disk
number on 8-bit systems (which came on both sides of 4 physical disks; unlike
DOS/Windows you have to physically flip over a two-sided floppy disk), which
the DOS version still treats as a virtual disk number.

Almost all other files have the extension DAX and are compressed archives; see
"DAX File Format" for details.

Data file overview (? is the virtual disk number):
8X8D?    8x8 symbol sets (8X8D1 includes the special case of the game font)
BACPAC   Wilderness map graphics 1
BODY?    Humanoid body graphics
CBODY    Character body combat icons
CHEAD    Character head combat icons
COMSPR   Common combat icons 1
CPIC?    Monster combat icons
DUNGCOM  Dungeon combat map icons
ECL?     ECL scripts (see Script Overview)
FINAL?   Endgame graphics
GEO?     3D maps (see 3D Map Format)
HEAD?    Humanoid head graphics
ICON     Wilderness 2D map party icon
ITEM?    Placed nonrandom item treasure (see Item Format)
ITEMS    Base items (see Base Item Format)
MON?CHA  Monster creature records (see Creature Format)
MON?ITM  Monster item records (see Item Format)
MON?SPC  Monster effect records (see Effect Format)
PIC?     Adventuring graphics
RANDCOM  Common combat icons 2
SPRIT?   Monster adventuring graphics
SQRPACI  Wilderness map graphics 2
TITLE    Title screen
WALLDEF? 3D map graphics
WILDCOM  Wilderness combat map icons

All graphics (every file above that does not refer to its format) are at some
level image buffers; see Image Buffer Format for details.

SECTION 12.2.1 - DAX File Format
--------------------------------

The DAX file format compresses multiple files in each DAX data archive file,
using a form of run-length encoding.

Header:
  * 2 bytes: 16-bit length of the rest of the header
  * Series of 9-byte file descriptors:
       Hex
    Offset  Function
        00  Number of this file (must be unique within this specific DAX)
     01-04  offset of the beginning of the file
     05-06  length of the decompressed file
     07-08  length of the compressed data

File (offsets do not count the header):
  First byte is the run length:
  * If run length 0...126:
    Copy the next (run length + 1) bytes to the decompressed file as-is.
  * If run length 129...255:
    Treat run length as signed 8-bit, then negate: repeat this number of the
    succeeding byte.
  * If run length 127...128:
    This causes an infinite loop; DAX file contents are expected to be well-
    behaved once loaded.
  If there are data left, the next byte is now the new run length.

This isn't very good even by 1988 standards, but it's fast and simple.

SECTION 12.2.2.1 - Creature Format
----------------------------------

This is for both characters (*.SAV in a saved game, *.CHA outside the party)
and monsters (stored in MON?CHA.DAX).  This also applies both on-disk and in
real memory (but not the GBVM).

A * indicates fields values that are defined, but not supported, by the game.
There are no fields for base values of the six stats, unlike all later Gold
  Box games.  Pool of Radiance resorts to some messy hacks to handle this,
  which can cause unexpected behavior.
For known spells (0x33...0x69), 0 means spell is not known, 1 means spell is
  known.  The number of the spell description is used in memorized spells
  (0x17...0x2B).
Pointers do not mean anything saved to disk, only in actual memory.

    Hex
 Offset  Function (offsets are in hexadecimal, values are in decimal)
000-00F  Character name (Pascal-encoded)
    010  Strength (current; does not include extraordinary Strength)
    011  Intelligence (current)
    012  Wisdom (current)
    013  Dexterity (current)
    014  Constitution (current)
    015  Charisma (current)
    016  Extraordinary strength (current; only valid when offset 0x10 = 18)
017-02B  Memorized spells (0 means no spell; anything else is a single spell;
         spell can be marked as to be memorized by setting bit 128)
    02C  Camp spell preparation base time in hours
    02D  Base 60 minus THAC0
    02E  Race:
           0 monster
           1 dwarf
           2 elf
           3 gnome
           4 half-elf
           5 halfling
           6* half-orc
           7 human
    02F  Class:
           0 cleric
           1* druid
           2 fighter
           3* paladin
           4* ranger
           5 magic-user
           6 thief
           7* monk
           8 cleric/fighter
           9 cleric/fighter/magic-user
           10* cleric/ranger
           11 cleric/magic-user
           12* cleric/thief
           13 fighter/magic-user
           14 fighter/thief
           15 fighter/magic-user/thief
           16 magic-user/thief
           17* monster
030-031  Age
    032  Maximum hit points
    033  Spell  1: C1 Bless
    034  Spell  2: C1 Curse
    035  Spell  3: C1 Cure Light Wounds
    036  Spell  4: C1 Cause Light Wounds
    037  Spell  5: C1 Detect Magic
    038  Spell  6: C1 Protection from Evil
    039  Spell  7: C1 Protection from Good
    03A  Spell  8: C1 Resist Cold
    03B  Spell  9: M1 Burning Hands
    03C  Spell 10: M1 Charm Person
    03D  Spell 11: M1 Detect Magic
    03E  Spell 12: M1 Enlarge
    03F  Spell 13: M1 Reduce
    040  Spell 14: M1 Friends
    041  Spell 15: M1 Magic Missile
    042  Spell 16: M1 Protection from Evil
    043  Spell 17: M1 Protection from Good
    044  Spell 18: M1 Read Magic
    045  Spell 19: M1 Shield
    046  Spell 20: M1 Shocking Grasp
    047  Spell 21: M1 Sleep
    048  Spell 22: C2 Find Traps
    049  Spell 23: C2 Hold Person
    04A  Spell 24: C2 Resist Fire
    04B  Spell 25: C2 Silence, 15' Radius
    04C  Spell 26: C2 Slow Poison
    04D  Spell 27: C2 Snake Charm
    04E  Spell 28: C2 Spiritual Hammer
    04F  Spell 29: M2 Detect Invisibility
    050  Spell 30: M2 Invisibility
    051  Spell 31: M2 Knock
    052  Spell 32: M2 Mirror Image
    053  Spell 33: M2 Ray of Enfeeblement
    054  Spell 34: M2 Stinking Cloud
    055  Spell 35: M2 Strength
    056  Spell 36: C3 Animate Dead
    057  Spell 37: C3 Cure Blindness
    058  Spell 38: C3 Cause Blindness
    059  Spell 39: C3 Cure Disease
    05A  Spell 40: C3 Cause Disease
    05B  Spell 41: C3 Dispel Magic
    05C  Spell 42: C3 Prayer
    05D  Spell 43: C3 Remove Curse
    05E  Spell 44: C3 Bestow Curse
    05F  Spell 45: M3 Blink
    060  Spell 46: M3 Dispel Magic
    061  Spell 47: M3 Fireball
    062  Spell 48: M3 Haste
    063  Spell 49: M3 Hold Person
    064  Spell 50: M3 Invisibility, 10' Radius
    065  Spell 51: M3 Lightning Bolt
    066  Spell 52: M3 Protection from Evil, 10' Radius
    067  Spell 53: M3 Protection from Good, 10' Radius
    068  Spell 54: M3 Protection from Normal Missiles
    069  Spell 55: M3 Slow
    06A  Spell 56: C7 Restoration
    06B  Sweep attack maximum targets
    06C  Lower 7 bits, icon size (1 = 1x1, 2 = 1x2, 3 = 2x1, 4 = 2x2)
         bit 128 = creature is Large or larger; for internal use only, this is
         set to 0 if the creature is out of play
    06D  Base saving throw vs. paralyzation, poison or death
    06E  Base saving throw vs. petrification or polymorph
    06F  Base saving throw vs. rod, staff or wand
    070  Base saving throw vs. breath weapon
    071  Base saving throw vs. spell
    072  Base movement
    073  Hit dice
    074  Drained levels (normally 0 for PCs, 255 for anything else)
    075  Drained hit points (normally 0 for PCs, 255 for anything else)
    076  Undead turning index (number indicates which line on table)
    077  Thief skill (includes all modifiers): Pick Pockets
    078  Thief skill (includes all modifiers): Open Locks
    079  Thief skill (includes all modifiers): Find/Remove Traps
    07A  Thief skill (includes all modifiers): Move Silently
    07B  Thief skill (includes all modifiers): Hide in Shadows
    07C  Thief skill (includes all modifiers): Hear Noise
    07D  Thief skill (includes all modifiers): Climb Walls
    07E  Thief skill (includes all modifiers): Read Languages
07F-082  Pointer to Effect linked list
    083  (unknown, never referenced, always 255 for monsters)
    084  Morale and NPC status; bit 128 is set if not a PC; 0 for PCs, 255 for
         monsters.  0 (PCs) and 179 can handle money normally.  NPC morale
         equals this minus 128 then divided by 2.  Friendly monsters that are
         not PCs or NPCs have this set to GBVM $6DC6.
    085  NPC treasure share (lower 3 bits only; upper 5 bits unknown and not
         ever referenced to my knowledge)
086-087  (unknown, never referenced, always 0 for monsters)
088-089  Copper coins
08A-08B  Silver coins
08C-08D  Electrum coins
08E-08F  Gold coins
090-091  Platinum coins
092-093  Gems
094-095  Jewelry
    096  Cleric level
    097* Druid level
    098  Fighter level
    099* Paladin level
    09A* Ranger level
    09B  Magic-user level
    09C  Thief level
    09D* Monk level
    09E  Gender:
           0 male
           1 female
    09F  Monster type (there are a few miscategorized monsters):
           0 humanoid
           1 monstrous humanoid
           2 giant
           3 dragon
           4 undead
           7 extra-dimensional
           8 fire-based
           9 cold-based
           10 regenerating
           11 non-snake reptile or amphibian
           12 avian
           14 snake or scorpion
           15 quadruped
           16 insect
           17 generic monster
    0A0  Alignment:
           0 lawful good
           1 lawful neutral
           2 lawful evil
           3 neutral good
           4 true neutral
           5 neutral evil
           6 chaotic good
           7 chaotic neutral
           8 chaotic evil
    0A1  Base number of primary attacks, multiplied by 2
    0A2  Base number of secondary attacks, multiplied by 2
    0A3  Base primary attack number of dice
    0A4  Base secondary attack number of dice
    0A5  Base primary attack dice sides
    0A6  Base secondary attack dice sides
    0A7  Base primary attack bonus damage (without modifiers)
    0A8  Base secondary attack bonus damage (without modifiers)
    0A9  Base 60 minus Armor Class
    0AA  Strength modifiers to-hit and to-damage allowed flag
    0AB  Randomized combat icon (not believed to be actually used)
0AC-0AF  Experience
    0B0  Item usability flags
    0B1  Maximum hit points without Constitution modifiers or "M"odify
    0B2  Cleric 1st level spell slots
    0B3  Cleric 2nd level spell slots
    0B4  Cleric 3rd level spell slots
    0B5  Magic-user 1st level spell slots
    0B6  Magic-user 2nd level spell slots
    0B7  Magic-user 3rd level spell slots
0B8-0B9  Experience award base for defeating
    0BA  Experience award per HP for defeating
    0BB  Portrait head (1...14)
    0BC  Portrait body (1...12)
    0BD  Icon head (0...13)
    0BE  Icon weapon (0...15)
    0BF  Icon animation index (0...25; not meaningful on disk)
    0C0  Icon height (1 = short, 2 = tall)
0C1-0C6  Icon colors
    0C7  Number of inventory items
0C8-0CB  Pointer to inventory (as linked list)
0CC-0CF  Pointer to equipped weapon
0D0-0D3  Pointer to equipped shield
0D4-0D7  Pointer to equipped body armor
0D8-0DB  Pointer to equipped gauntlets
0DC-0DF  Pointer to equipped helm
0E0-0E3  Pointer to equipped belt
0E4-0E7  Pointer to equipped robe
0E8-0EB  Pointer to equipped cloak
0EC-0EF  Pointer to equipped boots
0F0-0F3  Pointer to equipped ring 1
0F4-0F7  Pointer to equipped ring 2
0F8-0FB  Pointer to equipped arrow
0FC-0FF  Pointer to equipped bolt
    100  Hands used
    101  Saving throw bonus
102-103  Encumbrance in coins
104-107  Pointer to next Creature (creatures are organized as linked lists)
108-10B  Pointer to combat information
    10C  Status: (anything not listed here is an effect, not a status)
           0 okay
           1 animated
           2 tempgone
           3 running
           4 unconscious
           5 dying
           6 dead
           7 stoned
           8 gone
    10D  In play flag (mostly synonymous with status is Okay or Animated)
    10E  Hostility flag
    10F  Autocombat flag
    110  Current 60 minus THAC0
    111  Current 60 minus Armor Class
    112  Current 60 minus Armor Class for rear attacks
    113  Primary attacks remaining, multiplied by 2
    114  Secondary attacks remaining, multiplied by 2
    115  Current primary attack number of dice
    116  Current secondary attack number of dice
    117  Current primary attack dice sides
    118  Current secondary attack dice sides
    119  Current primary attack bonus damage
    11A  Current secondary attack bonus damage
    11B  Current hit points
    11C  Current movement

SECTION 12.2.2.2 - Statistics Minima and Maxima Table Format
------------------------------------------------------------

Each record is 16 bytes.

  Hex
Offs.  Meaning
   00  Strength minimum, male
   01  Strength minimum, female
   02  Strength maximum, male
   03  Strength maximum, female
   04  Extraordinary Strength maximum, male
   05  Extraordinary Strength maximum, female
   06  Intelligence minimum
   07  Intelligence maximum
   08  Wisdom minimum
   09  Wisdom maximum
   0A  Dexterity minimum
   0B  Dexterity maximum
   0C  Constitution minimum
   0D  Constitution maximum
   0E  Charisma minimum
   0F  Charisma maximum

SECTION 12.2.2.3 - Experience and Spells Table Format
-----------------------------------------------------

Each record is 56 bytes.  Entries are arranged first by level, beginning at
level 2, then by class.

* First 32 bytes:
  8 int32, for experience required (2...9; -1 means disallowed)
* Last 24 bytes:
  8 arrays of 3 uint8, for total spells per level (2...9)

SECTION 12.2.3 - Effect Format
------------------------------

Each record is 9 bytes; this is for both characters and monsters, and applies
both on-disk and in-memory.  On-disk storage is named *.SPC for characters, and
MON?SPC (inside MON?SPC.DAX) for monsters.

  Hex
Offs.  Function (offsets are in hexadecimal, values are in decimal)
   00  Effect ID: (specific spell effects are capitalized)
          1 Bless
          2 Curse
          3 wielding undead slaying weapon
          4 starting to train with Manual of Bodily Health (adds effect 7)
          5 Detect Magic
          6 wielding Flame Tongue weapon (the Flame Tongue sword fails to
            confer this effect)
          7 training with Manual of Modily Health
          8 Protection from Evil (either version)
          9 Protection from Good
         10 Resist Cold
         11 charmed
         12 Enlarge
         13 Reduce
         14 Friends
         15 Slow Poison damage
         16 Read Magic
         17 Shield
         18 gnome THAC0 bonus versus kobolds and goblins
         19 Find Traps
         20 Resist Fire
         21 Silence, 15' Radius
         22 Slow Poison expiration
         23 Spiritual Hammer
         24 see invisibility (works on 25 but not 71)
         25 invisibility
         26 dwarf THAC0 bonus versus half-orcs, goblins, hobgoblins, and orcs
         27 feather falling
         28 Mirror Image
         29 Ray of Enfeeblement
         30 coughing from Stinking Cloud
         31 helplessness (any source other than effects 51...53)
         32 Animate Dead
         33 blind
         34 diseased (causes effects 43 and 44)
         35 affected by allied Prayer
         36 Bestow Curse
         37 blinking
         38 extra strength
         39 hasted
         40 cast Stinking Cloud
         41 Protection from Normal Missiles
         42 slowed
         43 diseased (Strength drain)
         44 diseased (hit point drain)
         45 Protection from Evil, 10' Radius
         46 Protection from Good, 10' Radius
         47 dwarf/gnome AC bonus versus ogres, trolls, ogre magi, giants, and
            titans
         48 gnome AC bonus versus gnolls and bugbears
         49 Prayer
         50 mummy rot heal blocking (blocks most forms of healing; restores 1
            hit point every 10 days)
         51 Snake Charm
         52 held or paralyzed
         53 sleeping
         54 repulsed (unimplemented bronze dragon attack)
         55 poisoned
         56 wearing Ring of Invisibility
         57 mummy rot degeneration (lose 2 points of Charisma every 30 days;
            permanent death after 1d6 months)
         58 immobile
         59 add regeneration upon expiration
         60 unknown, unused
         61 wearing Ring of Fire Resistance
         62 regeneration from Constitution 20+
         63 unknown, unimplemented (must be able to suppress being targeted,
            but a handler doesn't exist)
         64 melee poison attack (unmodified saving throw)
         65 melee poison attack (+4 bonus to saving throw)
         66 melee poison attack (+2 bonus to saving throw)
         67 melee paralysis attack (save or be paralyzed for 2d8 minutes)
         68 melee paralysis attack (does not affect elves)
         69 melee paralysis attack (-2 penalty to saving throw, supposedly)
         70 melee poison attack (-2 penalty to saving throw)
         71 invisible (from dust; detect invisibility does not work on this)
         72 camouflaged (from Cloak of Elvenkind)
         73 rear claw rake (2 additional 2d4 attacks if 2 melee attacks hit)
         74 bite and hold process
         75 blood drained (1d4 damage per round, 12 damage total)
         76 melee blood draining attachment attack (blood drain and hold)
         77 melee bite and hold attack (bite attack holds defender in place)
         78 healed unconscious creature to positive HP during combat
         79 melee fire touch (2d10 additional fire damage)
         80 melee acid attack (1d4 additional acid damage)
         81 dragon fear aura (opponents with 3 or fewer HD must save vs. spell
            or be paralyzed indefinitely; opponents with 4...5 HD must save vs.
            spell or be cursed indefinitely)
         82 mummy fear aura (all opponents must save or be paralyzed; +1 bonus
            to save for party of 6 or more; +2 bonus to save for humans)
         83 petrifying gaze
         84 charming gaze (free Charm Person every round; -2 penalty to save)
         85 melee level-draining attack (1 level)
         86 melee level-draining attack (2 levels)
         87 melee mummy rot attack
         88 electrical breath attack (range 10, 100% chance in first round,
            50% chance thereafter, maximum 3 shots; area of effect as lightning
            bolt, max HP damage, save vs. breath weapon for half damage)
         89 displaced
         90 dwarf/halfling Constitution poison saving throw bonus (applies also
            to paralyzation/death)
         91 immunity to electricity and Magic Missile
         92 unknown, unused
         93 half damage from fire
         94 half damage from blunt or piercing weapons
         95 fighting on after being reduced to -6...0 hit points
         96 immunity to weapons that are neither silver nor magical
         97 dwarf/gnome/halfling Constitution spell/wand saving throw bonus
         98 regenerate 3 hit points per round
         99 keeps fighting after becoming unconscious (gain 1d4+1 minutes of
            95)
        100 troll vulnerability to fire and acid (only works if *all* damage is
            fire or acid); can return from death in 3d6 rounds otherwise
        101 troll add regeneration
        102 troll returning from going down
        103 can assume gaseous form
        104 thri-kreen missile evasion (60% chance of avoiding missile
            attacks)
        105 50% magic resistance (unused)
        106 85% magic resistance
        107 elven 90% resistance to sleep and charm spells
        108 immunity to sleep and charm
        109 immunity to paralysis (Hold Person and wand only)
        110 immunity to cold
        111 immunity to paralysis and poison
        112 immunity to fire
        113 efreeti fire resistance (-1 damage per fire damage die)
        114 half damage from electricity
        115 half damage from piercing or slashing weapons
        116 half damage from magical weapons
        117 vulnerability to holy water
        118 half damage from cold
        119 immunity to non-magical weapons (does not apply if attacker is not
            a PC race and has 4+ HD)
        120 boulder evasion (50% chance of avoiding thrown boulders)
        121 anhkheg acid squirt attack (25% chance of happening, max range 3,
            8d4 damage that isn't actually acid damage, save vs. breath weapon
            for half damage)
        122 mummy vulnerability to fire (magic or fire does +1 damage per die;
            3d8 damage from flasks of oil)
        123 immunity to weapons that are neither silver (half damage) nor
            magical
        124 half-elven 30% resistance to sleep and charm spells
        125 immunity to sleep, charm, paralysis, and poison
        126 suppressible gaze attack
        127 reflectable gaze
01-02 uint16 minutes remaining of effect (0 for permanent); exception:
         75 (blood drain): upper 4 bits of lower byte represent blood drain
            damage remaining
   03 255 indicates nothing special except immunity to Dispel Magic; if not:
      * If Strength-modifying effect (effect ID 12 or 38):
        If base Strength is 18, extraordinary Strength + 1
        If not, base Strength + 100
        Add 128 if this effect is weaker than another Strength-modifying effect
      * lower 4 bits are usually spellcasting level, for Dispel Magic.
        Exceptions (other than Strength effects):
          14 (Friends): base Charisma score
          28 (Mirror Image): number of images remaining
          75 (blood drain): index of target creature in creature array
      * upper 4 bits (for other than Strength effects):
          11 (charmed):
              bit 16 unknown (unreferenced)
              bit 32 flag for skipping applying new flags when applying effect
              bit 64 original hostility flag
          32 (Animate Dead):
              bit 16 original hostility flag
          39 (hasted):
              bit 16 aging effect applied flag
          40 (cast Stinking Cloud): Stinking Cloud index
          49 (Prayer):
              bit 16 hostility flag of caster
          57 (mummy rot degeneration): months remaining to live
          89 (displaced):
              bit 16 displacement attack prevention has triggered
      Dispel Magic checks the lower 4 bits if this isn't 255, which can cause
      unexpected behavior.
   04 Boolean if removing effect calls effect handler
05-08 Pointer to next Effect (a creature's effects are a linked list); not
      meaningful when stored on disk

Effects may be triggered given a condition type:
  0 suppress free actions (126)
  1 suppress being targeted (25, 37, 63, 71)
  2 attacker doing damage with primary attack (68, 73, 76, 77, 79, 80, 85...87)
  3 attacker doing damage with secondary attack (64...70, 79, 85...87)
  4 attacker has inflicted weapon damage (3, 6, 29)
  5 defender has taken weapon damage (28, 41, 60, 94, 96, 101, 104, 115...117,
    119, 120, 122, 123)
  6 damaged by non-weapon (10, 17, 20, 28, 60, 61, 90, 93, 101, 105, 106,
    112...114, 118, 122)
  7 helpless (31, 51, 52, 53)
  8 start of combat (56, 72, 81, 82, 89, 99)
  9 immune to effect (105...112, 124, 125)
 10 attacking with THAC0 roll (1...3, 6, 18, 26, 33, 36, 49)
 11 affects Armor Class (8, 9, 17, 30, 33, 45, 46)
 12 affects saving throw (8...10, 17, 20, 33, 36, 45, 46, 49, 61, 90, 97, 111,
    125)
 13 reduced to 0 HP (75, 76, 99, 100, 103)
 14 free actions (83, 84, 88, 121)
 15 start of combat action (11, 21, 30, 74, 75)
 16 defending against THAC0 roll (25, 37, 47, 48, 71, 89)
 17 affects morale (1, 2, 11)
 18 affects movement (39, 42, 58)
 19 end of combat round (11, 23, 56, 72, 98)

SECTION 12.2.4.1 - Item Format
------------------------------

This is for all of characters (*.ITM), placed treasures (inside ITEM?.DAX), and
monsters (inside MON?ITM.DAX).

Each item record is 63 bytes.

  Hex
Offs.  Function (offsets are in hexadecimal, values are in decimal)
00-28  Display name (Pascal String, lowercase OK)
   29  Boolean 0 for any Item (see String Linked List Member Format)
2A-2D  Pointer to next Item (inventories are linked lists in memory); not
       meaningful when stored on disk
   2E  uint8 Type
   2F  uint8 Identified name component 3
   30  uint8 Identified name component 2
   31  uint8 Identified name component 1
   32  int8 Magical bonus
   33  int8 Saving throw bonus
   34  Boolean Equipped (i.e., 0 = unequipped, 1 = equipped)
   35  uint8 Unidentified name components bitmask
   36  Boolean Cursed (i.e., 0 = uncursed, 1 = cursed)
37-38  uint16 Weight in coins
   39  uint8 Quantity (0 = single item that does not stack)
3A-3B  uint16 Item value in gold pieces
3C-3E  Properties:
         If item is scroll: 1...56 are spells (as in Creature File Format); if
           bit 128 is turned on, that spell is flagged for scribing
         If item is any other magical consumable:
           3C = number of charges
           3D = effect (if 80...90: subtract 23 for spell ID):
                 3 Potion of Extra Healing
                47 Wand of Fireballs
                51 Wand of Lightning (Bolt)
                80 Potion of Speed
                81 Keoghtum's Ointment
                82 Potion of Giant Strength
                83 Javelin of Lightning
                84 Wand of Paralyzation
                85 Potion of Healing
                86 Dust of Disappearance
                87 Necklace of Missiles
                88 Wand of Magic Missiles
                89 unknown unimplemented item
                90 Manual of Bodily Health
         Otherwise, check 3E (if not stated otherwise, confers any effect in
           3D if it is not 0):
           129 Ring of Fire Resistance
           131 Gauntlets of Ogre Power
           132 Aligned weapon; must have an alignment equal to the lower 4
               bits of 3D, or take damage equal to the upper 4 bits of 3D
           133 Cloak of Displacement
           134 Cloak of Elvenkind
           135 boulder
           136 Undead slaying weapon
           137 Spiritual Hammer
           139 Ring of Invisibility

SECTION 12.2.4.2 - Base Item Format
-----------------------------------

Base items are in the file named ITEMS, which has a 2-byte header (not ever
referenced in any fashion), followed by an array of 16-byte base item records:

  Hex
Offs.  Function (offsets are in hexadecimal, values are in decimal)
   00  uint8 Slot:
          0 main hand
          1 off hand (shield, mirror, or non-Bane holy symbol)
          2 body armor
          3 gauntlets
          4 helm
          5 belt or necklace
          6 robe
          7 cloak
          8 boots
          9 ring
         10 miscellaneous (including ammunition)
         11 magic-user scroll
         12 clerical scroll
         74 Leather Holy Symbol of Bane
         75 Brass Key
         77 bracers
         78 wand
         80 potion
         82 Efreeti Bottle
         91 Pass
   01  uint8 Hands required to use
   02  uint8 Damage vs. large, dice number
   03  uint8 Damage vs. large, dice sides
   04  int8 Damage vs. large, bonus
   05  uint8 Rate of fire for ranged weapons (multiplied by 2)
   06  uint8 AC adjustment descriptor: If 178 or higher, body armor or bracers
         bonus equal to the excess.  If not, but is 128 or higher, other AC
         bonus equal to the excess.  Values below 128 are ignored; an item must
         have an unignored value here to be eligible for a saving throw
         modifier.
   07  uint8 Weapon type:
           0 slashing
           1 piercing
         128 bashing
   08  unknown (not ever used to my knowledge)
   09  uint8 Damage vs. small/medium, dice number
   0A  uint8 Damage vs. small/medium, dice sides
   0B  int8 Damage vs. small/medium, bonus
   0C  uint8 Range of ranged weapon (exclusive)
   0D  uint8 Class usability bitmask:
         bit 1 magic-user
         bit 2 cleric
         bit 4 thief
         bit 8 fighter
         bit 16* druid
         bit 32* monk
         bit 64* paladin
         bit 128* ranger
   0E  uint8 Weapon properties bitmask:
         bit 1 shoots arrows
         bit 2 Dexterity missile bonus applies to weapon to-hit
         bit 4 Strength bonus applies both to weapon to-hit and weapon damage
         bit 8 AI will consider as primary ranged weapon
         bit 16 thrown
         bit 128 shoots bolts
   0F  unknown (always 0; suspected to be byte alignment)

SECTION 12.2.4.3 - Random Item Array Format
-------------------------------------------

Every integer is 16-bit integer, even if it is 8-bit in the regular item
format, and gets copied to the appropriate item offsets.

  Hex  Item
Offs. Offs.  Function
00-01    2F  Identified name component 3
02-03    30  Identified name component 2
04-05    31  Identified name component 1
06-07 37-38  Weight in coins
08-09 3A-3B  Item value in gold pieces
0A-0B    3C  Property 1
0C-0D    3D  Property 2
0E-0F    3E  Property 3

The following magic numbers (in decimal) are used both to calculate pointers
into the random item array and to distinguish randomly generated items:

  1 Potion of Extra Healing
  9 Potion of Giant Strength
 17 Potion of Healing
 25 Potion of Speed
 33 Scroll of Restoring Levels (code to generate randomly does not exist)
 41 Ring of Feather Falling
 49 Wand of Lightning (code to generate randomly cannot be reached)
 57 Wand of Magic Missiles
 65 Wand of Paralyzation
 73 Gauntlets of Ogre Power (code to generate randomly cannot be reached)
 81 Keoghtum's Ointment
 89 Necklace of Missiles
 97 Cloak of Displacement
105 Javelin of Lightning (code to generate randomly cannot be reached)
113 Wand of Fireballs
121 Ring of Fire Resistance
129 Ring of Invisibility
137 Bag of Holding
145 Cloak of Elvenkind

SECTION 12.2.5.1 - 3D Map Format
--------------------------------

The 3D maps are in GEO?.DAX, and are 1026 bytes.  It has a 2-byte header that
is always ignored, and is loaded to the reserved memory pointed to by DS:6A5C.

Each 3D map is a 16*16 array of 4-byte records:

  Hex
Offs.  Meaning (values are in decimal)
   00  Walls (north, east)
   01  Walls (south, west)
       A wall is a 4-bit integer.
   02  Event (0...255)
   03  Barriers for each direction (north, east, south, west)
       A barrier is a 2-bit integer:
         0 Impassable
         1 Passable
         2 Easy-to-open barred door
         3 Hard-to-open barred door

SECTION 12.2.5.2 - Wilderness Map Special Location Format
---------------------------------------------------------

For wilderness icons that require plot conditions to appear.

Each record is 6 bytes.

  Hex
Offs.  Meaning (values are in decimal)
00-01  Wilderness X-coordinate
02-03  Wilderness Y-coordinate
04-05  Wilderness tile ID

SECTION 12.2.6.1 - Combat Information Format
--------------------------------------------

Each record is 22 bytes.

  Hex
Offs.  Meaning (values are in decimal)
   00  uint8 Current spell ID being cast (0 = no spell)
   01  Boolean Casting allowed flag
   02  Boolean Item use allowed flag (applies only to AI)
   03  int8 initiative
   04  uint8 1 = primary attack, 2 = secondary attack
   05  unknown
   06  Movement times 2
   07  Boolean Guarding flag
   08  Boolean Made attack this round flag (not necessarily done this round)
   09  uint8 direction (0...7; I don't think this can be 8 or 255)
0A-0D  Pointer to other creature
   0E  uint8 Negative hit points
   0F  unknown
   10  Boolean Is turned by cleric flag
   11  Boolean Used turn undead this combat flag
   12  unknown
   13  Boolean Not PC or NPC flag
   14  Boolean Fleeing flag
   15  unknown (something to do with creature AI)

SECTION 12.2.6.2 - Combat Arena Format
--------------------------------------

This describes the combat arena aside from in-play creatures.  The separate
50*25 array at DS:60DB has creature indices.

Each record is 1257 bytes.

  Hex
Offs.  Meaning (values are in decimal)
00-01  unknown
   02  int8 Focus X-coordinate (0...49)
   03  int8 Focus Y-coordinate (0...24)
   04  Boolean unknown
   05  uint8 Focus icon size (1...4), actual creature not required
   06  unknown 8-bit
  07+  uint8 Combat unknown 1 index as 50*25 array; known values:
         31 PC/NPC on battlefield but out of play

SECTION 12.2.6.2 - Combat Arena Location Format
-----------------------------------------------
Each record is 4 bytes.

  Hex
Offs.  Meaning (values are in decimal)
   00  int8 X-coordinate (0...49), upper left for creatures taking >1 tile
   01  int8 Y-coordinate (0...24), upper left for creatures taking >1 tile
   02  unknown
   03  uint8 Icon size (1...4 or 0 for out of play)

SECTION 12.2.6.4 - Combat Target Data Format
--------------------------------------------

Each record is 3 bytes.

  Hex
Offs.  Meaning (values are in decimal)
   00  uint8 Creature index
   01  uint8 Range times 2
   02  unknown

SECTION 12.2.6.5 - Combat Unknown 1 Format
------------------------------------------

This is a constant array stored at DS:2748.

Each record is 4 bytes.

  Hex
Offs.  Meaning (values are in decimal)
   00  uint8 unknown (only possible values are 1 and 255; strongly suspected
       that 1 means passable and 255 means impassable, but I don't think it's
       only that, otherwise SSI would have just used a Boolean)
   01  uint8 unknown (always 0)
   02  uint8 unknown (always either 0 or 2)
   03  uint8 Tile ID

SECTION 12.2.6.6 - Combat Unknown 2 Format
------------------------------------------

Each record is 26 bytes.

  Hex
Offs.  Meaning (values are in decimal)
00-01  int16 X-coordinate A
02-03  int16 Y-coordinate A
04-05  int16 X-coordinate B
06-07  int16 Y-coordinate B
08-09  unknown 16-bit
0A-0B  int16 absolute value of (B - A) X-coordinate
0C-0D  int16 absolute value of (B - A) Y-coordinate
0E-0F  unknown 16-bit
10-11  unknown 16-bit
   12  int8 signum of (B - A) X-coordinate
   13  int8 signum of (B - A) Y-coordinate
14-15  unknown 16-bit
16-17  unknown 16-bit
   18  non-Boolean 8-bit
   19  unknown 8-bit

SECTION 12.2.6.7 - Combat Downed Character Format
-------------------------------------------------

Each record is 7 bytes.

  Hex
Offs.  Meaning (values are in decimal)
00-03  Pointer to Creature
   04  int8 X-coordinate
   05  int8 Y-coordinate
   06  uint8 Combat unknown 1 array index

SECTION 12.2.6.8 - Stinking Cloud Format
----------------------------------------

Each record is 19 bytes.

  Hex
Offs.  Meaning
00-03  Pointer to Creature who cast this Stinking Cloud
04-07  Pointer to next Stinking Cloud (these are stored as a linked list)
08-0B  unknown 8-bit non-Boolean array, 1-indexed, size 4
0C-0F  cloud exists Boolean array, 1-indexed, size 4
   10  int8 Combat arena upper left X-coordinate (0...49)
   11  int8 Combat arena upper left Y-coordinate (0...24)
   12  uint8 index

A Stinking Cloud does not contain information about its expiration; that is
handled by the expiration of an effect upon the caster.

SECTION 12.2.7 - Spell Format
-----------------------------

Each record is 16 bytes.

  Hex
Offs.  Meaning (values are in decimal)
   00  Type:
         0 clerical
         1 magic-user
         2 item
   01  Level of spell
   02  If 255, requires an attack roll; otherwise fixed range
   03  Variable range (multiply by spellcasting level)
   04  Fixed duration
   05  Variable duration (multiply by spellcasting level)
   06  Area of effect (may be adjusted by spell handler):
         0 caster
         4 1 target
         5 up to 2 targets
         6 up to 3 targets
         7 up to 4 targets
         8 any tile within line of sight
         9 diameter 3
        10 diameter 5
        11 diameter 7
       240 no limit to area; scales to caster level
   07  Target:
         0 combat
         1 caster
         2 any party member
         4 whole party
   08  Saving throw success result:
         0 no save allowed
         1 negate
         2 half damage
   09  Saving throw used:
         0 paralyzation, poison or death
         1 petrification or polymorph
         2 rod, staff or wand
         3 breath weapon
         4 spell
   0A  Effect added by spell (0 for none)
   0B  When spell can be cast:
         0 non-combat only
         1 combat only
         2 either
   0C  Spellcasting time
   0D  AI priority (0...7; higher is more likely, 0 is never cast by AI)
   0E  Boolean 1 if the spell is offensive, 0 otherwise
   0F  minimum number of targets required for the AI to cast spell

SECTION 12.2.8 - String Linked List Member Format
-------------------------------------------------

Each record is 46 bytes.  The String and the Pointer to the next String are in
the same locations as in the Item record, so an Item linked list may be read as
a String linked list.

 Hex
Offs.  Meaning
00-28  String
   29  Boolean title flag
2A-2D  Pointer to next String list member

SECTION 12.2.9.1 - Image Buffer Format
--------------------------------------

Each record is 23 bytes plus the size of the image data.

  Hex
Offs.  Meaning
00-01  Height of image in pixels
02-03  Width of image in chars (multiply by 8 for pixels)
04-05  unknown 16-bit
06-07  unknown 16-bit
   08  Images in buffer
09-10  Color map (EGA 16-color to CGA 4-color, as array of 16 nibbles)
11-12  Bytes in one image
13-16  Pointer to extra transparency space (default nil)
  17+  Image data

The size of the base storage space is height * width * images * (2 for CGA, 4
for EGA/Tandy graphics).  The memory allocation request is that plus 23.

If extra transparency space exists, it is equal to base storage for CGA/Tandy,
or 1/4 of base storage for EGA.  Bitmasks are negative bitmasks directly
applied to video memory writes.

For CGA, raw image data are converted to 4-color CGA using the color map,
with any transparent color set to 0.  There is no correction for CGA
interlacing (which is handled elsewhere), but entire rows can be copied as-is
to CGA video memory.

For EGA, raw image data have any transparent color set to 0, and blocks of 8
pixels are converted into 4 bytes of bit plane data; one byte is copied at a
time to EGA video memory.  (So 8 pixels of bit plane data at a time; not an
entire row of bit plane data all at once.)

Tandy graphics are simpler than either CGA or EGA and only require correction
for transparency.

If MCGA 320x200x256 support existed, it would be simpler than any of the above.

For images loaded from a file into an image buffer, this partial transformation
is done right away so the computations required are signficantly reduced.  I
don't believe the color map does anything at all after this.

An icon animation has two image buffers that are only animated actively as the
focus of the action.  Their size must be a multiple of 24 pixels in both
dimensions.

A tile set image buffer has multiple individual tile graphics.

SECTION 12.2.9.2 - Animation Data Format
----------------------------------------

Each record is 66 bytes.  Unlike an icon animation, this can handle up to 8
frames, and may be animated passively in the background.  A frame must be the
size of the 3D window view (88x88 pixels).

  Hes
Offs.  Meaning
   00  Number of frames in animation (maximum 8)
   01  Current frame (1-indexed)
02-41  Array of 8-byte subrecords, size 8:
       00-03 Duration
       04-07 Pointer to image buffer

SECTION 12.2.10 - Copy Protection Format
----------------------------------------

Each record is 21 bytes.

  Hex
Offs.  Meaning
00-06  Answer
07-08  Espruar rune
09-0A  Dethek rune (255 = do not print this character)
0B-14  Path

SECTION 12.3.1 - Script Overview
--------------------------------

The Gold Box games use an internal scripting language to drive events.  This
language is sophisticated enough to be Turing-complete; it's the earliest
computer role-playing game example I'm aware of.  (Games of the era typically
hardcode events in 6502 assembly.)  Infocom's Z-machine is also Turing-
complete, but Zork I is usually not considered a CRPG, even though it has a
combat engine and experience points.

The game scripts, written in Gold Box bytecode that changes a little from game
to game, are in the ECL?.DAX files, where ? is the disk number.  Scripts in
this game always are loaded at $9900 in the Gold Box Virtual Machine (GBVM),
and may not go past $B6FF, for a maximum script length of 7680 bytes.  It is
common for scripts to come close to this limit.  I often wonder what was cut to
fit everything in; there must be copious material that had to be cut for want
of space.

You can modify individual scripts and put them back in the DAX files, but this
may cause save file compatibility problems, as the current script is part of
the saved game.

The scripts are numbered as follows:
 0 Civilized Area (Main)
 1 Buccaneer Base
 2 Cadorna Textile House
 3 Valjevo Castle (Northwest and Southeast)
 4 Valjevo Castle (Northeast)
 5 Valjevo Castle Hedge Maze
 6 Valjevo Castle (Southwest)
 7 Valjevo Castle (Inner Tower)
 8 Civilized Area (Council)
 9 Stojanow Gate
10 Valhingen Graveyard
11 Civilized Area (Training Hall)
13 Kobold Caves
14 Kovel Mansion
15 Mendor's Library
16 Lizardman Keep (and Lizardman Keep Catacombs)
17 Nomad Camp
18 Podol Plaza
19 Silver Dragon Lair
20 Slums
21 Sokal Keep
22 Yarash's Pyramid Level 1
23 Yarash's Pyramid Level 2 and Level 3
24 Wealthy Area (and Temple of Bane)
25 Western Wilderness and Lairs
26 Central Wilderness and Lairs
27 Eastern Wilderness and Lairs
28 Zhentil Keep Outpost
29 Kuto's Well (and Kuto's Well Catacombs)

There are some scripts that cover multiple maps; these are the extra maps
covered:
30 Lizardman Keep Catacombs
31 Temple of Bane
32 Kuto's Well Catacombs

Script/map 12 does not exist.

Scripts always begin with five standard GBVM calling addresses:
1. vm_run_1         - Called by moving around on the map
2. search_location  - Called if move or "L"ook command is issued
3. pre_camp_check   - Called before entering camp main menu
4. camp_interrupted - Called if resting is interrupted
5. initial_entry    - Called after loading an ECL script or loading a game

SECTION 12.3.2 - Script Variable Types
--------------------------------------

All arguments for ECL script opcodes must explicitly declare their types.

GBVM addresses are always 16-bit, and should point to a recognized GBVM
address.  Most recognized addresses fall into one of these ranges:
  $4900...4CFF 16-bit bytes
  $6B00...6EFF 16-bit bytes
  $9700...98FF 16-bit bytes; scratch space for temporary variables
  $9900...B6FF 8-bit bytes; ECL script
  $C04B...C04F 8-bit bytes; current map location

Type Meaning
   0 8-bit integer value
     This is usually a uint8.

     The Gold Box games have multiple ways of handling signed 8-bit integers:
     * THAC0 and Armor Class are stored as (60 - number).
     * Sometimes, 128 is treated as 0.
     * Sometimes, the standard logic for signed 8-bit integers is used.

   1 GBVM address pointing to 8-bit integer value

   2 16-bit unsigned integer
     Always unsigned.  (If you're familar with Infocom's Z-machine, its
     integers are always signed 16-bit.)

   3 GBVM address pointing to 16-bit unsigned integer value

 128 GB string value
     * The first byte has the number of subsequent encoded bytes.
     * The subsequent bytes hold 6-bit values as follows:
       encoded bits: abcdefgh | ijklmnop | qrstuvwx
       decoded bits: abcdef | ghijkl | mnopqr | stuvwx
             0: ignored with no action (not a null terminator)
        1...31: add 64 (thus 1...26 represent A...Z)
       32...63: standard ASCII encoding
     GB string values are only valid within an ECL script, not anywhere else
     in the GBVM.

     Like Infocom's Z-machine, the purpose of this encoding is compression,
     not obfuscation.  The Gold Box games do not handle lowercase letters
     mostly to save space, as SSI's programmers often squeeze data very, very
     tightly.  Both of these are far inferior to short string compression
     algorithms even from the mid-90s, never mind modern algorithms like Smaz.

     Many other 1980s games encode strings not for compression but for
     obfuscation; examples include Magic Candle 1, Might & Magic 2, Phantasie
     1.

 129 GBVM address pointing to C string
     This never points to a GB string or a Pascal String.

SECTION 12.3.3 - Script Opcodes
-------------------------------

All ECL script opcodes are a single byte.  The names for the opcodes are those
given by debug mode in Curse of the Azure Bonds; Pool of Radiance v1.3 does not
have this debugging.

DS:6F7F contains the current ECL opcode.
The arrays at DS:7006, DS:7046, DS:7086, and DS:70C8 have any operands for
  that opcode.

A <var> can be either a value or a GBVM address; for the latter, the value
stored there will be retrieved (dereferenced).

00 EXIT
   Stops script execution, clears the ECL stack, and returns control to the
   player.

01 GOTO <address>
   Continues execution at <address>.

02 GOSUB <address>
   Continues execution at <address>, and pushes the current bytecode address to
   the stack.

03 COMPARE <var1> <var2>
   Compares <var1> and <var2> for a subsequent IF command.

04 ADD <var1> <var2> <address>
   The result <var1> + <var2> is placed at <address>.

05 SUBTRACT <var1> <var2> <address>
   The result of <var2> - <var1> is placed at <address>.  Yes, the subtrahend
   comes first.

06 DIVIDE <var1> <var2> <address>
   The result of <var1> / <var2> is placed at <address>.  This is integer
   division, with the dividend coming first.  The remainder is not saved,
   unlike in later Gold Box games.  Division by zero is not trapped.

07 MULTIPLY <var1> <var2> <address>
   Stores <var1> * <var2> at <address>.

08 RANDOM <var> <address>
   Generates a random integer from 0 to <var>, inclusive and maximum 255, then
   stores it at <address>.

09 SAVE <var> <address>
   Stores <var> at <address>.

0A LOAD CHARACTER <index>
   * If <index> is less than 128:
     Changes the current character using <index>; 0 is first in the marching
     order, and (party size - 1) is the last.  If <index> is at least equal to
     party size, monsters from LOAD MONSTER may be loaded.  References to
     $6B00...6C1B may now reference for the appropriate statistics; SAVE or
     SAVE TABLE for these actually change the character or monster.
   * If <index> is 128 or more:
     Monster (<index> - 128) will be placed on the party's side in the next
     combat.  If $6B00 and $6C00 are subsequently set to 0, this instead
     removes the monster; Pool of Radiance doesn't have the DUMP opcode all
     other Gold Box games have.

0B LOAD MONSTER <monsterID> <count> <pictureID>
   Adds <count> copies (minimum 1) of monster <monsterID> to the monster list,
   and loads graphic <pictureID> from CPIC?.DAX.  (<pictureID> is often equal
   to <monsterID>.)

0C SETUP MONSTER <monsterID> <distance> <pictureID>
   Starts a monster encounter with the primary monster <monsterID>,
   <distance> (0...2) squares away in the first-person view.  Determines actual
   distance of the monsters (they may be closer because of obstructions), and
   displays the appropriate monster graphic.

0D APPROACH
   The monsters from SETUP MONSTER close distance by 1 square.  If distance is
   0, this does nothing.

0E PICTURE <pictureID>
   * If <pictureID> is 255:
     Ends the graphical display and resumes the normal first-person view.
   * If $6DE1 is not 255:
     Displays a composite head and body graphic, using $6DE1 as the head in
     HEAD?.DAX and <pictureID> as the body in BODY?.DAX.
   * If $6DE1 is 255:
     Displays the specified picture from PIC?.DAX in the first-person view.  If
     there is more than one such picture, this displays an animation cycling
     through all such pictures.

0F INPUT NUMBER <maxDigits> <address>
   Asks the player to input a number with <maxDigits> maximum digits, and
   places it at <address>.

10 INPUT STRING <maxLength> <address>
   Asks the player to input a string with <maxLength> maximum characters, and
   places it at <address>.

11 PRINT <string>
   Prints <string> to the text box.

12 PRINTCLEAR <string>
   As above, but clears the text box first.

13 RETURN
   Returns from a GOSUB, with the next command after the most recent GOSUB
   pushed to the stack being executed.

14 COMPARE AND <var1> <var2> <var3> <var4>
   Compares <var1> to <var2> and <var3> to <var4> for a subsequent IF
   command.  Only IF = and IF <> are supported.

15 VERTICAL MENU <address> <message> <count> <stringVarargs>
   Displays <message> as a prompt in the text box, with the player able to
   select from the <count> options listed as <stringVarargs>.  The chosen
   option is placed at <address>, with 0 the first option and (<count> - 1)
   the last.

16 IF =
17 IF <>
18 IF <
19 IF >
1A IF <=
1B IF >=
   All six IF commands will skip the next command unless the most recent:
   * COMPARE has a true comparison
   * COMPARE AND has both comparisons true
   * AND has a true comparison to 0
   * OR has a true comparison to 0
   * FIND ITEM found a character carrying the appropriate item
   This means bit flags are checked with AND followed by an IF <>.

   This will not properly skip the following opcodes:
   * 0C SETUP MONSTER
   * 15 VERTICAL MENU
   * 25 ON GOTO
   * 26 ON GOSUB
   * 2B HORIZONTAL MENU
   * 36 ADD NPC

1C CLEARMONSTERS
   Resets hostile creature flags and resets all treasure.  Does not actually
   remove monsters from the creature list.

1D PARTYSTRENGTH <address>
   Calculates Party Strength using the calculation given in the Party Strength
   section, and places it at <address> as a uint8.

1E CHECKPARTY <attributeAddress> <effectID> <address1> <address2>
              <address3> <address4>
   * If attributeAddress is not 0 and effectID is 0:
     Checks all characters for the values at <attributeAddress>; only $6BA7
     (thief find/remove traps) and $6C1B (movement) are recognized.  Then:
     * <address1> is set to the lowest value
     * <address2> is set to the highest value
     * <address3> is set to the average value
     * <address4> is set to 0
   * If attributeAddress is 0 and effectID is not 0:
     Checks all characters for the given <effectID>.  Then <address4> will hold
     0 if no characters have the effect, or 1 if at least one character does.
     0 is written to the other three addresses.

1F undefined <unknown1> <unknown2>
   Something planned for, but unimplemented; all that remains behind is that it
   probably has 2 operands.

20 NEWECL <scriptID>
   Loads the indicated ECL script.

21 LOAD FILES <mapID> <unused> <suppressLoad>
   * If indoors, loads 3D map file without changing the ECL script.
   * If outdoors and <suppressLoad> is not 255, loads 2D map graphics from
     BACPAC.DAX.
   * In either case, refreshes the screen.

22 PARTY SURPRISE <address1> <address2>
   Initializes <address1> and <address2> for a subsequent SURPRISE command.
   <address1> is the modifier for the party to be surprised by the monsters.
   <address2> is the modifier for the party to surprise the monsters.
   These are both initialized to 0.  If different values are wanted for the
   party, they can be modified before the SURPRISE command.

23 SURPRISE <address1> <address2> <var1> <var2>
   Rolls for surprise, as set up by PARTY SURPRISE; <address1> and <address2>
   are as above.
   <var1> is the modifier for the monsters to surprise the party.
   <var2> is the modifier for the monsters to be surprised by the party.
   For each side, the overall modifiers are added to 2.  If a roll of 1d6 is
   less than or equal to this, that side achieves surprise.

   The modifiers aren't applied correctly; the actual calculations are:
     Monsters surprise party : 2 + <var2> - <address1>
     Party surprises monsters: 2 + <address2> - <var1>
   when it ought to be:
     Monsters surprise party : 2 + <var1> - <address1>
     Party surprises monsters: 2 + <address2> - <var2>
   This still works as expected with no modifiers, as is usually the case.

   The end result is placed at $6DCB:
     0 Neither side surprised
     1 Monsters surprise party
     2 Party surprises monsters
     3 Monsters and party surprise each other

24 COMBAT
   * Usually:
     Starts combat with the list of monsters from LOAD MONSTER.

     Combat can be modified by the following:
     * All monsters (including non-PC/NPC allies) have Morale of $6DC6.
     * If $6DCB is 1 or 2, the surprised side gets a -6 penalty to initiative
       in the first round of combat (which will always prevent any actions for
       any creature without a Dexterity bonus).
     * $6E70 is the int8 modifier to all monsters' THAC0.
     * $6E71 is the int8 modifier to all characters' THAC0.
     * $6E72 is the int8 modifier to all characters' movement.
     * The TREASURE command will load additional items that will show up as
       loot if the party wins.
     * If $6DE3 is 1, the monsters will not drop the items they are carrying.

     After combat:
     * $6DC7 is set to: 0 party won, 128 party lost, 129 party ran away.
     * $6DC8 is set to the number of monsters the party defeated.
     * $6E33 is set to 1 if the party attacked allies during the fight.
     * $6DCB, $6DE3, $6E70, $6E71, and $6E72 are all reset to 0.

   * If $6DE2 is 1:
     The party instead enters a temple.  $6DE2 is reset to 0 when done.

   * If $6E6C is 1:
     The party instead enters a shop.  $6E6C is reset to 0 when done.
     Available items for sale are what was loaded with the TREASURE command.
     Purchase prices would be multiplied by ($6E6D / 16), but $6E6D is always
     16 and this multiplier is only implemented in Curse of the Azure Bonds.

25 ON GOTO <var> <count> <addressVarargs>
   * If <var> is less than <count>, this acts as a GOTO command with the
     corresponding address from <addressVarargs>.
   * If <var> is equal to or greater than <count>, execution falls through to
     the next statement.

26 ON GOSUB <var> <count> <addressVarargs>
   Same as ON GOTO, except a GOSUB is executed.

27 TREASURE <copper> <silver> <electrum> <gold> <platinum>
            <gems> <jewelry> <treasureID>
   This adds all the indicated treasure for the next COMBAT.
   If treasure ID is:
     *   0...127: all the items from that list are added.
     * 128...254: <treasureID> minus 128 random magical items are added.
     *       255: no items are added.

28 ROB <isWholeParty> <percentMoney> <itemChance>
   Takes money and items away.
   * If <isWholeParty> is 0, affects only the current character;
   * If <isWholeParty> is 1, affects all characters.

   All affected characters always lose <percentMoney>% money.

   For <itemChance>, consider every item:
   * If item is less than 2.5 pounds, <itemChance>% chance of loss
   * If item is 2.5 to 25.5 pounds, (<itemChance> - 50)% chance of loss
   * If item is more than 25.5 pounds, (<itemChance> - 90)% chance of loss
   You can't lose a medium item if <itemChance> is below 50, and you can't
   lose a heavy item if <itemChance> is below 90.

   The logic for robbing items has multiple problems:
   * There is no check to see if the item is equipped; there's nothing stopping
     a sword from getting stolen right out of your hand.
   * For each character, itemChance is reduced for a medium or heavy item, and
     is not restored until the next character.  That means you can dramatically
     reduce the odds of item theft by putting your heaviest items at the top of
     your inventory.

29 ENCOUNTER MENU <monsterID> <distance> <pictureID> <address>
                  <combat> <wait> <flee> <advance> <parley>
                  <message0> <message1> <message2>
                  <movement1> <movement2>
   Starts an encounter with the monster with <monsterID> and <pictureID>,
   starting <distance> squares away.  The messages displayed in the text box
   are for the given distance from the party; if no message is defined, the
   message for lower distance is used.

   <combat>, <wait>, <flee>, <advance>, <parley> all define the monsters'
   response if you choose that action:
     0 combat
     1 do whatever you chose (monsters will always wait if you do)
     2 monsters flee (unless you attacked them; the monsters will only escape
       the party if <movement2> is greater than your fastest party member)
     3 monsters advance
     4 parley
   Your party does not "F"lee unless you choose to do so, which succeeds if
   the movement of your slowest party member is at least <movement1>.

   If the party successfully advanced, the menu is repeated.

   The resolution is placed at <address>:
     0 monsters flee
     1 combat begins
     2 party flees
     3 parley

2A GETTABLE <address1> <var> <address2>
   Retrieves the value from address <address1 + var> and places it at
   <address2>.  Equivalent to retrieving a value from an array.

2B HORIZONTAL MENU <address> <count> <stringVarargs>
   Similar to VERTICAL MENU, but the message to the player must be printed
   beforehand, and the string choices are displayed on the bottom menu bar.
   The player can additionally select an option with the first letter of the
   string.

   Scripted "Press <enter>/<return> to continue" prompts use this in earlier
   Gold Box games.  "P" will work for these prompts; an address must still be
   supplied, which can cause bugs.

2C PARLAY [sic] <haughty> <sly> <nice> <meek> <abusive> <address>
   Gives the player the option to choose a parley attitude; the value
   defined for the chosen option is placed at <address>.

2D CALL <address>
   Re-interprets <address> as a function (on an 8-bit system this might be an
   actual direct machine code subroutine call); none of these addresses are
   otherwise valid in the GBVM.  Recognized <address> values:
   $2C90 redraw first-person view
   $8000 character vs. clone duel setup
   $8001 character vs. monsters fight setup
   $BA03 play sound effect 10 (if GBVM $03DE is 8) or 11 (if it is 10)
   $C018 refresh GBVM $C04E current wall
   $C01E move 1 square forward (barriers are ignored), wrapping around and
         refreshing current event and wall

   Any other <address> results in a no-op.

2E DAMAGE <attack> <dice> <sides> <bonus> <var>
   This does damage equal to <dice>d<sides> + <bonus>.
   If bit 128 of <attack> is not set:
     This executes <attack> physical attacks on random characters, with a THAC0
     of (60 - <var>).  Damage is rerolled for every character.
   If bit 128 of <attack> is set, damage is only rolled once:
     Who gets damaged depends on <attack> bit 64:
     * If set, the entire party takes damage.
     * If not set, but <var> bit 128 is, a random character takes damage.
     * Otherwise, the currently chosen character takes damage.

     A saving throw is not allowed if:
     * the entire party was attacked *and* <attack> bit 32 is set; or
     * the current character is attacked (not randomly) and the specified save
       type is vs. paralyzation, poison or death.
     Otherwise, a saving throw to avoid all damage is, with a bonus of <attack>
     lower 5 bits, of type <var> lower 3 bits:
       0 saving throw vs. paralyzation, poison or death
       1 saving throw vs. petrification or polymorph
       2 saving throw vs. rod, staff or wand
       3 saving throw vs. breath weapon
       4 saving throw vs. spell

2F AND <var1> <var2> <address>
   The result of a bitwise AND on <var1> and <var2> is placed at <address>.
   This is used to read bit flags.  Comparison flags are also set up for a
   subsequent IF = or IF <>.

30 OR <var1> <var2> <address>
   The result of a bitwise OR on <var1> and <var2> is placed at <address>.
   This is used to set bit flags, though ADD is also used.  Comparison flags
   are also set up for a subsequent IF = or IF <>; I can't think of any script
   that actually does this, though that is normal in machine code.

31 SPRITE OFF

32 FIND ITEM <itemID>
   This checks all party members for an item with the given <itemID> for a
   subsequent IF.  Only IF = and IF <> are supported.

33 PRINT RETURN
   Prints a single blank line to the message box.

34 ECL CLOCK <var>
   Never actually used.  Time passes, always in minutes (unlike later games).
   The number of minutes should be <var>, but instead uses the index of an
   uninitialized uint8.  (Fixed and used in Curse of the Azure Bonds.)

35 SAVE TABLE <var1> <address> <var2>
   Reverse of GETTABLE; places <var1> at <address + var2>.  Equivalent to
   storing a value in an array.

36 ADD NPC <monsterID> <morale>
   Adds the given monster to your party, with the indicated <morale>.  NPC is
   designated friendly, with the hardcoded exception of LEVEL 6 MU (#24).

37 LOAD PIECES <walldefID> <unknown> <unknown>
   Loads 3D map graphics.

38 PROGRAM <program>
   Runs a special routine depending on <program>:
     0 training hall (using the class bitmask in $6DA8)
     8 win game
     9 camp
   Any other <program> is a no-op.

39 WHO <message>
   Shows <message> and has the player select a party member, who also gets
   LOAD CHARACTER executed.

3A DELAY

3B SPELL <spellID> <address1> <address2>
   In principle, searches for a character with the given spell:
   * if no such character exists: <address1> will hold 255;
   * if such a character does exist:
     <address1> will hold the index of the spell slot;
     <address2> will hold the index of the character;
     also, a LOAD CHARACTER <address2> command is executed.
   In practice, this opcode is always a no-op.  (Fixed in Curse of the Azure
   Bonds.)

3C PROTECTION <address>
   Prints a runic phrase from <address>.

3D CLEAR BOX
   Clears the message window.

SECTION 12.4.1 - Script Flag List
---------------------------------

The game scripts use 256 16-bit flags ($4A00...4AFF in the game scripts) to
hold most non-character game state.  They are typically treated as unsigned
8-bit integers, even though they are effectively 16-bit bytes.

$4A00...4A1F are reset to 0 every time a new ECL script is loaded.
$4A20...4AFF are not automatically reset; sometimes the game scripts do.

An asterisk after the address means that GBVM address has special hardcoded
handling outside of any ECL script.

In the save file (SAVGAM?.DAT; ? is the letter of the save), $4A00 corresponds
to 0x201 in the save file.  So to convert the below addresses to save file
addresses:
  (1) subtract 0x4A00
  (2) multiply by 2
  (3) add 0x201

$4A00 Shared flag:
      Slums: 255 got message from entering map
      Sokal Keep 1: 255 destroyed elf skeleton
      Mendor's Library 2: 1 philosophy section entered
      Kovel Mansion T3: 1 trap encountered
      Yarash's Pyramid Level 1 13: 1 found book
      Stojanow Gate 9: 1 encountered man with wagon
      Valjevo Castle Inner Tower 7: 1 Genheeris's office entered
$4A01 Shared flag:
      Civilized Area 2: 1 party now allowed to board boat
      Podal Plaza: number of base monsters in random encounter
      Mendor's Library 3: 1 mathematics section entered
      Kovel Mansion T4: 1 trap encountered
      Kobold Caves 1: 1 dealt with pit
      Buccaneer Base: 1 saw heir to Bivant
      Valhingen Graveyard: count of new random skeletons for current visit
      Stojanow Gate:
        bit 1 location 1 entered
        bit 2 location 2 entered
$4A02 Shared flag:
      Podal Plaza: random encounter base monster ID
      Mendor's Library 4: 1 history section entered
      Kovel Mansion T5: 1 trap encountered
      Temple of Bane 7: 1 destroyed altar to Bane
      Cadorna Textile House 5: 255 hobgoblins defeated
      Kobold Caves 2: 1 found kobold's drawing
      Valhingen Graveyard: count of new random zombies for current visit
$4A03 Shared flag:
      Sokal Keep: random encounter counter
      Podal Plaza: random encounter number of monster leaders
      Mendor's Library 5: 1 rhetoric section entered
      Kovel Mansion T6: 1 trap encountered
      Kobold Caves 3:
        1 kobold escorts party
        2 deadfall trap disarmed
      Buccaneer Base 6: 255 purchased pass

      255 Kuto's Well cleared (set to this if this value reaches 10)

SECTION 12.4.2 - Other Script Addresses
---------------------------------------

These GBVM addresses are also referenced in the ECL scripts.  They are not to
be confused with DS addresses.

A "character" is the current character: either currently selected in the UI,
or specified by the LOAD CHARACTER or WHO commands.

$00B1 unknown
$00B8 unknown
$00B9 unknown
$00FB unknown
$00FC unknown
$033D Direction, including diagonals:
        0 north
        1 northeast
        2 east
        3 southeast
        4 south
        5 southwest
        6 west
        7 northwest
        (in some other contexts, either 8 or 255 mean no direction)
$03DE Sound effect
$49C3 X-coordinate (outdoors)
$49C4 Y-coordinate (outdoors)
$49C5 Map ID
$49C6 Segment of the minute (not ever used)
$49C7 Minute of the turn (minutes ones digit)
$49C8 Turn of the hour (minutes tens digit)
$49C9 Hour of the day
$49CA Day of the month
$49CB Month of the year
$49CC Elapsed years (poorly behaved after 256 years elapse)
$49E5 1 cannot cast spells
$49E6 Outdoors flag (0 = indoors, 1 = outdoors)
$49E7 unknown 3D map graphics variable
$49E8 unknown 3D map graphics variable
$49EB unknown
$49F0 X-coordinate (previous)
$49F1 Y-coordinate (previous)
$49F2 Previous ECL script ID
$49FB 1 Automap will not work
$49FC Game speed
$49FD Color of sky (converted to actual color using DS:0C26 map).  11 is sky
      blue either normally or after map conversion.  I don't know how this
      works on an 8-bit system; 11 defaults to pink on an Apple ][.
$49FE Color of ceiling (converted to actual color using DS:C026 map)
$49FF bit 1 Portraits setting
      bit 2 Monster pics setting

(see CALL opcode description for details).

SECTION 12.5 - Save File Format
-------------------------------

The save game procedure first assures the GBVM is up-to-date:
  * DS:6AB2...6AB3 -> GBVM $4AF9
  * DS:6AB6...6AB7 -> GBVM $4AFA
  * DS:6ABA...6ABB -> GBVM $4AFB
  * DS:6AB4...6AB5 -> GBVM $4AFC
  * DS:6AB6...6AB7 -> GBVM $4AFD
  * DS:6ABA...6ABB -> GBVM $4AFE
  * DS:49E2        -> GBVM $49FC
  * DS:49F5        -> GBVM $49FF bit 1
  * DS:49F6        -> GBVM $49FF bit 2
  * DS:5376        -> GBVM $6E12

It then performs the following writes to SAVGAM?.DAT (? is the save letter):
  Bytes  Description
      1  current virtual disk number (should be the same as GBVM $6E12)
   2048  GBVM $4900...4CFF (16-bit bytes)
   2048  GBVM $6B00...6EFF (16-bit bytes)
   1024  GBVM $9700...98FF (16-bit bytes)
   7680  GBVM $9900...B6FF (8-bit bytes)
      5  GBVM $C04B...C04F (8-bit bytes)
      1  DS:49FA party location type
      1  DS:49F3 interface state
      1  party size (should be the same as GBVM $6E3E)
    328  size-8 array of length-40 Strings CHRDAT?# (# is character index)
  -----
  13137

Characters are saved to CHRDAT?#.SAV by writing the 285-byte character record
as-is.

Items are saved to CHRDAT?#.ITM by writing every 63-byte item record as-is, one
after another.

Effects are saved to CHRDAT?#.SPC by writing every 9-byte effect record as-is,
one after another.

The most prominent data that are not saved are anything related to the state of
barred doors.  The save game file includes the entire current ECL script; I
don't know what SSI's justification for that is.

SECTION 12.6.1 - Executable Overview
------------------------------------

All discussion of the executable assumes you are running PC version v1.3.  It
very rarely matters which version of v1.3 (original CD version vs. any cracked
CD version like the GOG release).

There is no 64KB limit on the amount of executable code anywhere; in Turbo
Pascal CS can and does regularly change depending on code branch.

Turbo Pascal uses as a calling convention:
  * parameters are pushed to the stack left-to-right
  * consequently, parameters appear on the stack upside-down
  * callee is responsible for restoring the stack pointer
All of these are reversed from the typical C/C++ calling convention.  (This
convention makes it impossible for Write/Writeln to work like C's printf at the
machine code level.)  One thing that does work like C/C++: stack variables are
never automatically set to 0.

Turbo Pascal regularly places data in CS, in both library code and compiled
code.  Other compilers are more strict about keeping data out of CS.  (In other
words: the typical DOS compiler keeps code in the code segment, and data in the
data segment, but Turbo Pascal doesn't adhere to that convention.)

The Turbo Pascal v6 compiler seldom generates the following:
* code to push/pop registers to use registers as temporary variables within a
  compiled function.  Its library machine code often does this, but its
  compiled code rarely does; SI and BX are often available but unused.
* machine code jump tables (SSI implements their own jump tables regularly;
  it's just the compiler they used that doesn't have that kind of savoir-faire)

The stack size is the Turbo Pascal default 16384 bytes.  SS should never
change; a stack of >64KB is both awkward and colossal by 1980s standards.  DS
should be mostly constant; it may change during a library call, or when the
programmer explicitly requests it.  ES fluctuates a lot, as is generally the
case.

Machine code is arranged by code segment, both by address and by IDA's decimal
indexing.

Stated segment addresses are what both the code and IDA say they are, but this
will not reflect the actual segments during program execution.

Parameters are passed on the stack unless otherwise specified.

Turbo Pascal is a pass-by-value language, more so than C; an array will not
decay to a pointer unless the programmer specifies it.  (It's still not pure
pass-by-value, as a File may not be passed by value.)  As pass-by-value is the
default, Strings are often passed by value: the compiler reserves stack memory,
copies the parameter to the stack, then passes a far pointer to that copy.
This maintains pass-by-value semantics, but comes at the cost of large amounts
of machine code and execution time spent copying Strings.

Returns on the stack are possible; it is caller's responsibility to handle
the stack being unwound, typically by passing the return as parameters to a
subsequent function call.

Nested function/procedure declarations are allowed, unlike in C; all
unshadowed arguments and variables of the enclosing scope are visible to the
nested function/procedure, via shared stack space.  (If this sounds like a
combination of convenient, error-prone, and hard-to-analyze, I agree.)

Compiler flags known to have been used by SSI:
  * {$E+} (8087 emulation)
    The Gold Box games use some floating-point, and Pool of Radiance was
    written to run on an 8088, so floating-point emulation is required.  There
    is a bit of unreferenced 8087 machine code present, which is an artifact of
    the compiler not being that fine-grained.
  * {$G-} globally (80286 code generation)
    Pool of Radiance was written to run on an 8088.
  * {$I+} while loading the game; often {$I-} thereafter
    If a disk error happens while setting up the game, an {$I+} fatal error
    isn't unexpected.  Once gameplay begins, SSI sometimes switches over to
    {$I-}; the best way for a Turbo Pascal programmer to check if the right
    virtual disk is present is to try to open then close a file on the desired
    disk.  If {$I+} were always active, Pool of Radiance would quit to DOS if
    this check fails.
  * {$N-} (numeric processing)
    Pool of Radiance doesn't use >48-bit floating-point.
  * {$O+} (overlay code generation)
    Pool of Radiance has enough code that it swaps code in and out using
    overlays stored in GAME.OVR.
  * {$R-} globally (range check function is unreferenced)
    Constantly range checking would incur a performance penalty.  This is how
    Constitution outside 3...20 causes strange behavior; if {$R+} were the
    case, Pool of Radiance would quit to DOS.
  * {$S-} globally (stack overflow check function is unreferenced)
    Constantly checking for stack overflow would incur a performance penalty.

Compiler flags unknown:
  * {$A} (align data)
    The Turbo Pascal documentation claims that data is either always aligned or
    never aligned.  It's actually sometimes aligned and sometimes not, so the
    documentation must be wrong.  Most likely, this is {$A-}, which is what I
    would have written; {$A+} would improve performance on 80286+ computers
    while worsening performance on an 8088, and that's the opposite of what SSI
    was clearly aiming for.  I don't know for certain, though.
  * {$B} (force full Boolean evaluation)
    Likely {$B-} (Boolean short-circuiting) globally; having a good reason to
    desire {$B+} is rare, and is outright not allowed in C.  (C++ can require
    {$B+} if you are overloading binary boolean operators, which is a flaw in
    C++ typically fixed by code standards banning that.)

SECTION 12.6.2 - Executable Code
--------------------------------

(Lib) means that the function is part of the Turbo Pascal library, not anything
SSI wrote -- in these cases, the machine code IS the source code, more or less.
Internal parameters often but do not necessarily match what SSI's programmers
would have seen.  This code is optimized for speed, not space or readability.

(Unref) means that IDA Pro believes the function is not referenced.  If a
function is referenced only by unreferenced functions, that is considered
referenced by IDA Pro; if a function is referenced only via pointer, that is
considered unreferenced.

(Unexec) is my own belief of whether a function never actually executes.

(Wrap) means this is a wrapper function (e.g., for trapping division by zero).

Many empty functions below look like initialization functions where no
initialization is necessary; they're always at the tail end of a unit.

Turbo Pascal functions and procedures are documented in TPv6LR.

For graphics, SSI rolled their own code directly interacting with hardware; the
Graph unit is actually completely absent.  The Graph unit of Turbo Pascal had
no Tandy support, which would have been a problem for writing a commercial game
in 1988, but even v4 did have EGA and custom font support.

Segment 0000 (IDA segment 0): (uncompressed EXE - add 200 for offset)
Main.  Not actually called "main", unlike C/C++; also not treated as a normal
function.  Main is treated as a normal function in C but not C++; if this were
a C program, main would be called from here.  (Reminder: this code won't
actually be at 0000:0000 during program execution.)
0000 0005 "STING"
0006 0150 Main
          (* Call initializer for most units.  Most are empty functions; the
             ones that are not:
             * Segment 50, System
             * Segment 48, Crt
             * Segment 40, game initialization 1
             * Overlay 1, out of memory handling
           * Call 26B3:002D game initialization 2.
           * Call 18FE:0627 introduction.
           * Call 197F:00FD copy protection, if STING cheat not used.
           * Call 3171:0161 main menu.
           * Call 19B5:32F8 main game loop.)
0151 015F (segment byte alignment)

Segment 00BA (IDA segment 26): (uncompressed EXE - add 1480 for offset)
Commonly used functions.
Begin End
0000 01FA Creature weapon statistic adjustment
          Parameter: Pointer to Creature
          (Modifies creature statistics according to its currently equipped
          weapon.  Accounts for weapon statistics, Strength, Dexterity, magical
          pluses on both weapon and relevant ammunition, and the elven +1 bonus
          to-hit with some weapons.  Does nothing if creature has no equipped
          weapon.)
01FB 0281 Creature armor movement adjustment
          Parameter 1: Pointer to Creature
          Parameter 2: Pointer to Item
          (Accounts for magical plus on armor, but does not adjust for anything
          other than armor.)
0282 039F Creature armor adjustment helper
          Parameter 1: Pointer to Creature
          Parameter 2: Pointer to Item
          Parameter 3: Pointer to int8[5] armor adjustment
                         0: Dexterity
                         1: shield
                         2: miscellaneous
                         3: Ring of Protection
                         4: body armor or bracers
          Parameter 4: Pointer to Boolean item is magical body armor
          Return at Pointer to int8[5]: armor adjustments for item
          Return at Pointer to Boolean: if body armor with +1 or higher bonus
          (Item may be any equippable item that can affect AC.  Primarily
          mutates the provided armor adjustment array; the only changes

Segment 0709 (IDA segment 42): (uncompressed EXE - add 7970 for offset)
Begin End
0071 009F Setup EGA current page
          Parameter: uint8 page (0...7)
          (Switches the current page and calculates its offset.  Multiple EGA
          pages were apparently planned, then abandoned by SSI; the page is
          always 0.  These functions don't actually calculate the offset of a
          page correctly; an EGA 320x200x16 page isn't 512 bytes, it's 8000.
          That doesn't matter because the page is always 0, but what does
          matter is that many EGA writes are performed twice for no reason.)
00A0 00B6 Setup EGA other page
          Parameter: uint8 page (0...7)
          (As above, but with no call to INT 10 AH 05.)
0171 0176 "8x8d1"
0177 017B ".dax"
017C 01DE Load game font (from 8X8D1.DAX)
01DF 05EA Print char on screen using game font
          Parameter 1: uint8 column (does nothing if >= 40)
          Parameter 2: uint8 row (does nothing if >= 25)
          Parameter 3: uint8 color (0...15)
          Parameter 4: uint8 count (number of times to repeat this char)
          Parameter 5: uint8 char code
          Parameter 6: Boolean isASCII (0 means GB character set)
05EB 0625 Print spaces
          Parameter 1: uint8 column
          Parameter 2: uint8 row
          Parameter 3: uint8 color
          Parameter 4: uint8 count of spaces
0626 06A6 Print String on screen using game font
          Parameter 1: uint8 column (does nothing if >= 40)
          Parameter 2: uint8 row (does nothing if >= 40)
          Parameter 3: uint8 color (0...15)
          Parameter 4: Pointer to String

          0768)
