the southeastern Valjevo Castle Hedge Maze) are useful, but are not referenced
anywhere in the game.

SECTION 14.2 - Novelization
---------------------------

The party that undertakes the quest in the novel is:

Shal (human female fighter/magic-user)
Tarl (human male fighter/cleric)
Ren (human male ranger/thief)

They are multi-classed and equipped with very powerful magical items for low
level characters to make up for the fact that there are only ever three of
them.  Yes, that's the same Shal that later shows up in Pools of Darkness, the
game.  The three are also the main characters in Pools of Darkness, the
novelization.

Tavern brawls are even deadlier in the novel than in the Gold Box games!

Only the following quests are depicted: Sokal Keep; Cadorna Textile House;
Yarash's Pyramid; Valhingen Graveyard; Valjevo Castle Hedge Maze and Inner
Tower.

The Gold Box version, the tabletop version, and the novelization are all
independent of each other.  This is not true for Curse of the Azure Bonds or
Pools of Darkness.

SECTION 14.3 - Historical Accuracy
----------------------------------

Yes, I know, no one is seriously going to think this game is realistic, but I
like to talk about this.  Besides, Pool of Radiance and Curse of the Azure
Bonds (though not the later Gold Box games) were clearly written by people who
knew lots of historical detail, and who am I not to acknowledge that?

* Standard D&D 1E has a technology level of AD 1400 plus or minus 15 years,
  without the gunpowder weapons that existed at the time.  Even 1420 starts to
  have technology beyond what is supposed to exist; 1440 is right out, as
  that's about when Gutenberg introduced his printing press (though these do
  exist far away from the Moonsea region).  Gunpowder (which doesn't work in
  the Forgotten Realms) also starts to have a significant effect as the 15th
  century progresses: cannon played a significant role in the siege of Orleans
  (late 1420s), and a decisive one in the fall of Constantinople (1453).
  Firearms take several more decades to become significant, starting around the
  Battle of Cerignola (1503).

  Later D&D editions tend not to care about historical accuracy as much as
  1E/2E.  The reason for this is partly the last point on this list, and partly
  because most people would perceive a realistic treatment as unrealistic --
  they actually expect a fake version of history, as the standard fantasy
  setting contains numerous elements from the modern era.

* Werner von Urslingen was an actual historical character, known for leading a
  fearsome mercenary army in the 14th century.

* Bishop Braccio is likely meant to suggest Braccio da Montone, who was a
  military commander in the early 15th century.  (I don't believe he was a
  clergyman.)

* Porphyrys Cadorna is likely meant to suggest Luigi Cadorna, the terrible
  Italian World War I general.  "Terrible": I think George "at least I'm not as
  awful as Daniel Sickles" McClellan could general circles around Cadorna.

* Valjevo is the name of a good-sized city in Serbia.  (I don't believe it has
  any significant surviving fortress like Stirling Castle or the Red Fort.)

* The entire conception of several-on-several organic fair combat isn't
  particularly realistic.  Even if you set aside the questions of "should I
  fight those guys at all" and "how do I know those are actually the people I
  want to fight", you don't want a fair fight.  You structure everything around
  getting the jump on your enemy, and not getting jumped: avoid a fair fight to
  begin with.

  You can in principle build games around this.  However, that makes your game
  hinge more upon jumping the other side than actual combat, so D&D generally
  doesn't do more than a few perfunctory modifiers to some simple dice rolls.
  That causes problems for decent gameplay (it's much worse in earlier Wizardry
  than here), as fairness now hinges upon those rolls.  That kind of realism
  has thus typically been abandoned these days (to a significant extent even in
  any later Gold Box game).

  Arranged several-on-several combat, such as gladiatorial combat, trial by
  combat, or duels, can work, but that also mostly goes against the traditional
  D&D combat model.  The relative importance of duels in Pool of Radiance
  compared to later Gold Box games may have been another attempt at realism.

  This problem tapers off if combat is at least around battalion-scale, but I
  have never heard of a single-player RPG of such scale where you can control
  individual combatants.  Surprise is often a major factor even at larger
  scales; my standard examples are the Battle of Lake Trasimene (217 BC) and
  the Battle of Okehazama (1560).

* A single gold piece is actually a lot of money.  A precise conversion to
  present-day currency isn't possible (this also applies in real life if more
  than a few decades have passed), but at any point in time it's very roughly
  equivalent in value to a decent set of formal clothing, today perhaps several
  hundred USD/Euro.  So if you're paying 1 platinum piece for a room, that's a
  few thousand USD/Euro.  Even with the inflated prices a frontier economy
  would be expected to have, that's probably too high (for realism purposes;
  it's fine for gameplay, as you don't have to maintain food stocks).

  Trying to convert in-game weight to spot gold prices gives a much higher
  value, but that only reflects how D&D 1E doesn't understand how much actual
  coins weigh.  Gary Gygax clearly did not just weigh, say, an actual denarius,
  or Roman silver piece, which is not hard to come by compared to an actual
  aureus, or Roman gold piece.

  Also, gold is currently worth significantly more than platinum.

* Melee weapons are far lighter in real life than they are in D&D 1E, and many
  other games copied its super-heavy weapon model.  A standard D&D 1E long
  sword weighs 6 pounds.  It's not practical to use a one-handed weapon heavier
  than about 5 pounds at most, and an actual long sword weighs about 3 pounds
  (which is the 5E value).  For comparison, a modern baseball bat is definitely
  a two-handed weapon, but it weighs only about 2 pounds.

  In the other direction, a long sword weighs 13.0 pounds in Angband and 19.6
  pounds in Ultima VI.  Even those don't touch the absurdity of Robert
  Baratheon's warhammer, which Eddard Stark (not a cripple) can barely even
  lift.

  D&D 1E/2E weapon weights do actually have a justification, to reflect the
  practical headache of actually carrying around a long sword, but games
  typically don't consider that.

* "Long sword" is something of a gaming English term; a better real-world term
  is "arming sword".  A real-world "long sword" corresponds roughly to a type
  of a gaming English "two-handed sword" in older games, though it's becoming
  more common for a game "long sword" to actually require two hands.

* Broad swords actually are from the early modern era, about the mid-1500s at
  the earliest.  They were intended to accompany firearms.

* Real swords are often blunter than people think.  This is actually
  deliberate; late medieval and early modern European long swords were designed
  so that they could be held two-handed with one hand on the blade (this
  technique is called "half-swording"), specifically to change the weapon from
  a slashing one to a thrusting or bashing one -- slashing is virtually useless
  against a well-armored opponent, but thrusting and bashing still remain
  useful.  (Swordmaking in Japan placed an emphasis on sharpness partly because
  good armor was significantly rarer there than in Europe.)

  Yes, you aren't supposed to slash at a heavily-armored opponent.  Aside from
  half-swording, you can also grapple or overbear such an opponent, wield a
  weapon (e.g., a warhammer) effective against good armor, go after the more
  numerous lightly-armored opponents, or aim at the horse the well-armored
  opponent is often riding.

* Those flails your cleric wields are of dubious historical authenticity.
  There is a notable lack of references to them in older literature, and it's
  suspected that the handful of examples in museums are fraudulent.

* Polearms were used by militaries for much longer than people think; the
  United States military did not fully stop using them until around 1900.
  (... No, bayonets do not count as polearms.)

* Crossbows are supposed to be much easier to use than regular bows; that's why
  they had such an impact in real life.  This does happen to some extent in
  later editions of D&D, just not 1E/2E.  For a skilled hero, a bow would be
  better because it has a higher rate of fire; reloading a medieval European
  crossbow takes a long time.  A Chinese repeating crossbow (often written
  "cho-ko-nu" in gaming English) is even easier to use.

* Medieval crossbows or regular bows have serious logistics problems that games
  rarely consider.

  A crossbow takes a long time to reload, but you do need to in a real battle.
  A European crossbowman is typically accompanied by a shieldbearer carrying a
  gigantic shield (a pavis, much larger than a tower shield) that the
  crossbowman can shelter behind to reload.  That's double the number of men
  your army's quartermaster needs to feed, right there.

  A longbowman might achieve a rate of fire of 10 shots per minute -- 2.5x that
  of character with Haste in this game.  The problem is supplying that much
  ammunition.  A longbowman isn't carrying 250 arrows like your characters
  might; it will be more like several dozen, not enough to last more than a few
  minutes.  That longbowman is going to need wagons with ample supplies, and
  even those can be exhausted given that a battle can take hours; commanders
  may be faced with having to send those hard-to-train longbowmen into melee
  combat.  Even paying for that many fletchers to make that many arrows turns
  out to be a significant expense ("Fletcher" is a not uncommon last name for a
  reason) -- also, how are you going to move that many arrows?

  Also, if you read Lord of the Rings, did you notice how Tolkien sedulously
  tracks Legolas's supply of arrows?  It's fairly significant that these games
  track individual arrows at all.

* Assuming that skill is not a problem (not a reasonable assumption for actual
  armies, but fine for a handful of heroes), bows are actually powerful enough
  that they aren't fully superseded by firearms until the appearance of mass-
  produced rifles in the mid-19th century.  On the other hand, bows are not as
  good at punching through plate mail/armor as commonly believed; the reason
  the English won a crushing victory at the Battle of Agincourt (1415) wasn't
  because longbows work like wands of magic missiles.

* Slings are actually much deadlier than people (and D&D rules) generally
  think.  People tend to think of slingshots when slings are mentioned; those
  are genuinely weak.  Slings are extremely difficult to actually aim well,
  though; it's entirely possible to accidentally hurl a sling stone backwards,
  which is unlikely with a bow or gun no matter how much of a new user you are.
  (That means David taking out Goliath is more a matter of superior skill than
  of being a fortunate underdog.)

* It's commonly said that real plate mail isn't nearly as limiting as CRPGs and
  standard fantasy worlds portray them.  That isn't really the case in D&D
  1E/2E, which make a distinction between "plate mail" and "plate armor".
  (Yes, that's weird, but the rules explicitly state it.)  "Plate armor" is
  usually what people mean when they say "plate mail", and it's "plate armor"
  that is easy to move around in, very protective, and supremely expensive.
  "Plate armor" protects the entire body with very few gaps, using interlocking
  overlapping plates to do so, and distributing the weight so it's not as
  encumbering.  It's also, according to the 1E DMG, too technologically
  advanced to exist in a standard D&D game world.  It was invented later
  (about 1420) than gunpowder weaponry, being developed partly to counter it.
  Only in the 17th century, during the Thirty Years' War, did gunpowder weapons
  finally become powerful enough to make plate armor not worthwhile in combat.

  If you want to look up the formal modern academic terms for plate armor,
  start with "white armor" and "Gothic plate armor"; the difference between
  these is more important than knowing the difference between a glaive and a
  glaive-guisarme.

* In modern usage, "mail" is only supposed to describe armor made of linked
  rings.  So "chain mail" is still a valid term, but "plate mail" is not.

* It's doubtful whether "ring mail" and "banded mail" even ever existed at all,
  historically.  Even the D&D 2E PHB reports this, but by then people had
  already been throwing around the 1E terms for years.  Gygax cites a source
  from 1909.  (Reminder: it once was difficult to find up-to-date sources.)

* Leather armor did exist, but it wasn't actually used often, because it
  doesn't work well in practice.  This is partly because cloth/padded armor
  actually works better than most people think.

* Shields really ought to protect you better in combat than they do here.  A 5%
  reduction in hit chance is little compared to how much it actually helped.
  This is less true if you have the more technologically advanced plate armor
  around; once armor that good started appearing, elite fighters relied less
  and less upon shields -- but armor that good isn't supposed to exist in D&D
  1E/2E.

* It's stated in the sourcebooks (e.g., the Wilderness Survival Guide) that
  lower-level lightning spells aren't as deadly as the real thing.  The reverse
  is actually the case, since in real life, a direct lightning bolt strike only
  kills the victim about 10% of the time.  In D&D 1E/2E, a Lightning Bolt that
  does 5d6 damage will outright kill a person with 10 hit points (more than the
  median soldier) more often than that.

* While pin-and-tumbler locks predate the entire medieval era, they were rather
  exotic items until the Industrial Revolution, especially with the invention
  and spread of the mass-produced Yale lock in the mid-19th century.  Their
  presence is sensible here if they're protecting something valuable, but in
  many CRPGs, including this one, they often protect things of little or even
  no value.  In such cases, it may actually make economic sense to steal the
  lock itself.

* Even major cities in the Forgotten Realms have low populations; there are few
  cities with a population of more than 50,000.  The same is true of most other
  D&D settings.  Real life and typical fantasy settings both have their largest
  cities with populations in the hundreds of thousands.

  In 1400, Paris was the largest western European city, with a population of
  about 250,000.  The next largest cities would have all been in what is now
  northern Italy.  This is all despite a severe famine that started in 1315
  (roughly the same time the Little Ice Age began and reduced crop yields), the
  Hundred Years' War which started in 1337, and the Black Death which started
  in 1348.

  I suspect the low populations of the typical D&D setting reflects its more
  direct inheritance from Tolkien compared to most other fantasy fiction.
  Middle-earth in the late Third Age is similarly depopulated, just with
  clearer reasons for it.

* People these days tend to assume the literacy rate was very low before the
  invention of Gutenberg's printing press.  It was still around 15% to 20% in
  Western Europe.

* The level of trade depicted in the Gold Box games is more typical of the
  Renaissance or early modern age than the actual late medieval period.

* Inns were also not significant in the medieval era, because there weren't
  enough travelers to justify very many of them.  Travelers tended to rely upon
  one of two alternatives.  (1) Travelers often just relied upon individual
  hospitality; this is partly why sacred hospitality used to be such a big
  deal.  (2) Many travelers in the medieval era were actually religious
  pilgrims, and often stayed at monasteries.  Gygax was aware of this (the 1E
  MM/DMG random encounter tables have you encounter pilgrims fairly often), but
  most of his imitators are not.

  This is related to the above point, because trade results in more travelers.
  The same also applies to a significant extent to taverns.  Not that inns or
  taverns didn't exist at all -- they're a notable element of the Canterbury
  Tales (1386) -- but they were not common.  (You'll probably realize that this
  statement also depends on exactly when you define the medieval era ending.)

* While you don't see it too much in Pool of Radiance, Forgotten Realms
  political entities like Cormyr or Sembia behave an awful lot like modern-day
  Westphalian nation-states, which shouldn't happen in a pre-modern culture
  without good reason.  (Though that is not necessarily unrealistic -- the
  Roman Empire behaved in many ways like a modern nation-state.  It just isn't
  anything like medieval Europe.)

* Similar to the above, those political entities also tend to have significant
  standing armies -- very much like the Romans did, and very much unlike actual
  European medieval powers; very much like most other standard fantasy nations,
  and very much unlike Westeros.

* If you've read a lot of printed fantasy, you may notice there isn't much
  reference to feudalism in D&D.  This actually accurately reflects the time
  period; western European feudalism started to break down as early as the 12th
  century, and that decline accelerated in the 14th century.  There are
  multiple reasons for this that historians debate furiously, even including
  "feudalism never existed", but probably the most important is the
  centralization of power in kings, which itself happened for all kinds of
  reasons.  The main practical problems with feudalism are the lack of a good
  way to stop two major vassals from fighting each other, and that vassals
  tended to prefer being taxed to the mandatory military service standard for
  feudalism.

  The Black Death in the mid-14th century particularly sped up the decay of
  western European feudalism: many workers died, but capital was preserved;
  thus demand for labor vastly outstripped supply, leading surviving commoners
  to demand, and often actually get, better living conditions.  This in turn
  helped eventually lead to commoners having enough wealth to actually justify
  the numerous inns and taverns found in the standard fantasy setting.

  Feudalism is actually more visible in Tolkien (who kept it muted, because he
  was actually an anarcho-monarchist ... yes, Tolkien actually considered
  himself an anarchist), and in Martin (in Westeros, society typically works on
  an early medieval societal model, despite the latest technology often being
  early modern or even 19th century), than it is in almost all CRPGs.

* Castles stopped being built for military purposes when cannons got good
  enough.  Dragons, earth elementals, and teleportation should also count
  towards this, really.  (A lot of real castles were actually built for
  aesthetic purposes, because "late medieval is awesome!" has been popular for
  a long time -- Neuschwanstein is probably the best example of this.  That's
  the castle the one in Disneyland is based upon.)

* Food supplies are generally not an issue in the standard fantasy world; when
  it does become a problem, it's usually because of a war.  In real life, they
  were a problem even for peacetime affluent societies all the way up to the
  19th century.  There were massive volcanic eruptions in 1783 and 1815, either
  more significant than any since, that caused global cooling significant
  enough to cause crop failures in the following years.  This was one of the
  driving forces behind westward expansion in the United States; farmland in
  marginal areas was abandoned at the time.  (Those forests in New England that
  are now famous for changing colors in the fall?  Those were mostly farmland
  when the United States declared independence.)

  In the real medieval period, you could expect crop failures resulting in
  famine every several years; a single crop failure generally didn't result in
  a severe famine, but two or more in a row was another matter.

* In the Gold Box games, wolves generally have a bad reputation, as they do in
  much of standard fantasy.  They generally have a much better reputation these
  days, with much of that because they are the sigil of the Starks of Westeros.
  This is likely inspired by real-life western Europe having problems with
  wolves terrorizing urban citizens all the way up to about the 18th century.

* Any magical society with a sufficiently advanced level of magic is going to
  use it as we do technology; you will start to get things like crystal ball
  telecommunications networks and social teleportation services.  This DOES
  happen in Eberron and Harry Potter; the Forgotten Realms also have a high
  magic level and should thus behave in many un-medieval ways.  There's a
  reason why Tolkien, Martin, etc. mostly create low-magic worlds; powerful
  magic like continents sinking into the ocean and 700-foot-high 300-mile-long
  enchanted ice walls should be decidedly exceptional, or it will make your
  world behave untraditionally.

  This is also a reason why Greyhawk is typically the standard D&D game
  setting: Dark Sun, Eberron, Planescape, and Ravenloft are all deliberately
  un-medieval in many ways, and Dragonlance has its own idiosyncratic
  mythology.

* A "buccaneer" is supposed to be specific to the Caribbean and its age of
  piracy.  "Pirate" is a better culture- and era-independent term.  The Tampa
  Bay Buccaneers are in the right general area, at least.  The ones in this
  game ... not so much.

* Finally, the very concept of not having anachronisms in fiction is itself a
  modern one.  You won't find much like it in Shakespeare, for instance.  It
  only starts to be significant around the time of the popularity of Ivanhoe,
  published in 1819.  Tolkien didn't care much; his societies actually most
  resemble 6th-century England, so many of the things we take for granted in
  fantasy fiction would fit there about as much as express trains, cruise
  missiles, or armored personnel carriers would in AD&D -- and all three of
  those actually DO exist in Middle-earth, incidentally.  (Tolkien was always
  cheerful about anachronisms.)  And when you get to computer games, you have
  battery-powered brass lanterns in the original Adventure, plastic explosives
  in Zork, space fighter combat in Ultima, blaster guns in Might and Magic,
  elevators in Wizardry, tinker gnomes in Krynn, the church of Gond
  Wonderbringer in the Forbidden Realms ... and even if you insist on
  historical accuracy, which relatively few non-modern games even try to do,
  it's very hard to prevent technology from centuries later getting through
  somewhere.  (For instance, doorknobs were only invented during the
  Industrial Revolution -- but almost the first thing mentioned in The Hobbit
  is the knob on the door to Bag End.)

SECTION 14.4 - Miscellaneous Criticism
--------------------------------------

I'm not going to do a full review here (if I didn't think this game was good,
do you think I'd be writing all this?), but do want to make some remarks that
don't often come up.

* One often overlooked phenomenon in determining why an original piece of media
  is often good compared to its sequels: You have much more time to write the
  first installation than any subsequent one.

  No one truly knows about the first installation in your series until it hits
  the shelves, after all.  Once it is published, now you're under pressure to
  produce a sequel in less time than it took to produce the original.

  To meet that schedule, for a game it's often easy to adapt the same engine.
  This does actually make sequels less work, but any deficiencies in the
  initial engine are magnified, and even without that other problems crop up;
  the writers of the Gold Box games are not quite as literate following Curse
  of the Azure Bonds.

  It's also easy to correct problems in sequels without adequately considering
  what made the original good.  I believe that's a large part of what makes
  Secret of the Silver Blades not nearly as good as any other games in this
  series.

* The Gold Box engine went through numerous refinements, but one thing that
  was never dropped is the 7680 byte script limit.  Even with graphics taking
  twice the space, greatly enlarging that 7680 should not have been much of a
  technical problem once you drop support for any 8-bit platform.  That limit,
  already a problem here, becomes worse in later games.

* Those character portraits ... did you notice they don't even appear for most
  of the game?  They only work if you are on virtual disk 3; the documentation
  actually says this, but it's easy to miss.  And then did you notice they
  don't depict more than one race ... for multiple definitions of the term
  "race"?  It's an obvious thing to drop if your resources are in any way
  limited (SSI did exactly this); this is about the start of the era where the
  number of floppy disks your program fits becomes a serious limit.

* Am I the only one annoyed SSI didn't know how to spell "parley"?  They were
  clearly more literate than that ...

* SSI clearly had well-above-average programmers; look at how Secret of the
  Silver Blades works under the hood.  But they'd probably get torn to shreds
  if you transplant modern practices to 1990.  A lot of what they wrote here
  could easily be shot down either by a technical interviewer or in code
  review.

  Back then, if you were truly the elite of the elite, you often wouldn't write
  a game in a language as high-level as Pascal or C; you'd write in assembly.
  The main reason you would not, portability, was a serious problem circa 1990.

* There were very good reasons to replicate the D&D tabletop game engine.
  People tend to think it's for some combination of familiarity and
  marketability; that explains much of but not all of it.

  It also takes significant resources to develop game mechanics that are
  interesting and well-balanced.

  Wizardry 1/2/3 work in part simply because their authors ruthlessly stole
  from D&D (the entire concept of "originality" was hardly known that long ago,
  anywhere in software; almost everyone stole from everyone else).

  Ultima works because Origin builds worlds, as its ads say.  They certainly
  don't build well-rounded game mechanics.

  Might and Magic works because Jon van Caneghem essentially said "all of this
  is awful" and wrote something that wasn't.

  If you copy D&D's known-to-not-be-awful mechanics, you skip a huge amount of
  work and can put more resources into actually scripting the game.

  My standard example of a contemporary original system which was thought over
  but nonetheless has severe flaws is Dragon Wars.  There, if you invest in the
  wrong skills, you may be screwed and have to start over.  Also, you are
  *more* likely to be killed with higher maximum hit points.

* The Gold Box games have a major advantage over most other contemporary CRPGs:
  it mostly applies the same rules to everyone, whether party or monster.  SSI
  could have made that a major selling point.  "Sick of monsters shooting
  diagonally when you cannot?  Tired of monsters having endless reserves of
  magic?  Fed up with your characters having hundreds of hit points when enemy
  humans have thousands?"

  A lot of that is simply just good programming practice: why write two
  separate code paths for characters and monsters, when you can just write it
  once?


SECTION 15.1 - References
-------------------------

Here are some other sources you can consult about the game.

AD&D Pool of Radiance Clue Book, ISBN not known (I suspect there isn't one), 64
  pp.  This has a complete set of maps, is reasonably comprehensive, and is the
  classic reference for the game.  Physical copies are of some interest to
  collectors.  Despite being published by SSI, this isn't actually all that
  accurate, though it is still far more accurate than the average Prima
  strategy guide.  Its details appear to better reflect the Apple II or
  Commodore 64 versions of the game.

These three books are the core D&D 1E books that have many of the basic rules:
  AD&D 1st Edition Player's Handbook, ISBN 0-935696-01-6. (PHB)
  AD&D 1st Edition Dungeon Master's Guide, ISBN 0-935696-02-4. (DMG)
  AD&D 1st Edition Monster Manual, ISBN 0-935696-00-8. (MM)
Any source that claims these games primarily use BECMI or D&D 2E is wrong.  The
2E rules are relatively similar to 1E rules (they're the pair of editions that
are most closely related), but significant differences still exist.  At the
time of Pool of Radiance's release, OD&D/BECMI/1E were all that existed.  Later
Gold Box games still use mostly D&D 1E rules even though 2E was then the latest
and greatest in the tabletop world.

AD&D 1st Edition Unearthed Arcana, ISBN 088038-084-5. (UA)  These optional
  rules released later in 1st Edition's lifecycle are mostly not used in the
  Gold Box games, though there are a few exceptions, primarily magical items.

AD&D 1st Edition Monster Manual II, ISBN 0-88038-031-4. (MM2) This has
  several relevant monsters not described in the original Monster Manual, and
  also the rules for statistics of 19 and higher.

AD&D 1st Edition Field Folio, ISBN 0-935696-21-0.  (FF)  While all the other
  D&D Gold Box games draw some inspiration from here, and some monsters
  featured in other CRPGs made their debut here, nothing from this book is in
  Pool of Radiance.

AD&D 1st Edition Deities & Demigods, ISBN 0-935696-22-9.  (DD)  This has the
  rules for statistics above 18.  I don't think it has anything else relevant.
  (DD does clearly spell out the magic resistance rules; the DMG also has them,
  but good luck locating them!)

AD&D Forgotten Realms Campaign Set (1st Edition), ISBN 0-88038-472-7.  This has
  the original campaign information for the Forgotten Realms.

AD&D Forgotten Realms Ruins of Adventure Official Game Adventure, ISBN
  0-88038-588-X.  (RoA)  This is the official tabletop game adaptation of the
  computer game.  It follows the same rough overall plot, but there are many
  differences in detail.  It has a reputation for being nowhere near as good as
  the computer game, mostly because it doesn't take advantage of the additional
  interaction a human DM can provide.  It is also generally easier than the
  Gold Box version in principle, perhaps because the computer version has the
  potent magic of "quit and load saved game".

Pool of Radiance, by James M. Ward and Jane Cooper Hong, ISBN 0-88038-735-1,
  316 pp.  This is the official novelization of the computer game.  Like the
  adventure module, its reputation is nowhere near as good as the computer
  game.

Quest for Clues II, ed. Shay Addams, ISBN 0-929373-01-04 (same as for QfC III
  which is odd), 181 pp.  This is the Quest for Clues book with a Pool of
  Radiance walkthrough, on pages 83...93.  As with all Questbusters
  walkthroughs, it isn't particularly detailed.

Computer Gaming World references:
  #49 (Aug 1988) p. 20...21: Sneak preview.
  #54 (Dec 1988) p. 28, 60...62: Two reviews, by Ken St. Andre and Tracie
    Forman Hines; many criticisms, both positive and negative, look strange
    in retrospect.  Scorpia never gave a full-length review, even though she
    played and completed the game.
  #55 (Jan 1989) p. 33: early sales reports.
  #64 (Oct 1989) p. 41: nominee for 1989 RPG of the Year.
  #72 (Jun 1990) p. 18: yes, it really was that slow on an original IBM PC.
  #76 (Nov 1990) p. 70: yes, the AI really was that bad in earlier versions.
  #85 (Aug 1991) p. 51: Scorpia gives a hint about Tyranthraxus.
  #87 (Oct 1991) p. 20: Scorpia gives a brief review in her RPG survey.

  CGW's reader ratings initially were high, though not as high as Magic Candle,
  Ultima V, or Wasteland.  (You should play all of those, too!)

  Minor mentions (including ads):
  #38 (Jul-Aug 1987) p. 42
  #42 (Dec 1987) p. 23
  #44 (Feb 1988) p. 36
  #45 (Mar 1988) p. 17
  #48 (Jun 1988) p. 57
  #49 (Aug 1988) p. 10
  #50 (Sep 1988) p. 22, p. 46
  #52 (Oct 1988) p. 38
  #53 (Nov 1988) p. 50
  #56 (Feb 1989) p. 63
  #60 (Jun 1989) p. 8...9
  #61 (Jul 1989) p. 40...41
  #62 (Aug 1989) p. 8...9
  #66 (Dec 1989) p. 69
  #67 (Jan 1990) p. 86
  #68 (Feb 1990) p. 64
  #77 (Dec 1990) p. 127

QuestBusters references:
  #47 (Vol. V #10, Oct 1988) p. 1, 11...13: review by Shay Addams.  Mentions
    it's combat-heavy and faithful to the tabletop version.  It also says it's
    fast on a Commodore 64.
  #49 (Vol. V #12, Dec 1988) p. 14: user-written hints.  And by user, I mean
    Ken St. Andre, best known for Wasteland.
  #51 (Vol. VI #2, Feb 1989) p. 2: announcement of IBM conversion
  There was never a full-scale walkthrough published in QuestBusters; it only
    appeared in Quest for Clues II.

  Minor mentions (includes user-written hints, normal for QuestBusters):
  #48 (Vol. V #11, Nov 1988) p. 14
  #50 (Vol. VI #1, Jan 1989) p. 13...14
  #51 (Vol. VI #2, Feb 1989) p. 13...14
  #52 (Vol. VI #3, Mar 1989) p. 2
  #53 (Vol. VI #4, Apr 1989) p. 2
  #57 (Vol. VI #8, Aug 1989) p. 2
  #58 (Vol. VI #9, Sep 1989) p. 13
  #61 (Vol. VI #12, Dec 1989), p. 13

http://gbc.zorbus.net/index.html
  Gold Box Companion home page.  Already listed elsewhere, but this page also
  has links to many other Gold Box Web pages worth mentioning.

http://www.gamebanshee.com/poolofradiance/
  Polished and reasonably comprehensive Web-based walkthrough.

http://www.gamefaqs.com/
  nes/587518-advanced-dungeons-and-dragons-pool-of-radiance/faqs/51292
  This is the best walkthrough I know of for the NES version.

https://rpgcodex.net/forums/threads/
  lets-play-pool-of-radiance-with-one-character.129308/
  Null Null's single-character playthrough.  (Yes, this is possible.)

http://crpgaddict.blogspot.com/
  CRPG Addict has extensive coverage of Pool of Radiance (significantly more
  than what he has tagged).

For more technical references, see Hacking Overview.

SECTION 15.2 - FAQ Version History
----------------------------------

This is a changelog.  Any older significant revision should be available via
the Internet Archive.

v2.00 (2026-04-02)
* This latest deep dive fixes a lot of mistakes and adds even more detail.  A
  large majority of such places where I just trusted (1) the documentation; (2)
  the clue book; (3) what the game says; (4) the D&D 1E sourcebooks; or (5) how
  later Gold Box games work, where that information does not reflect what the
  game actually does, should now be fixed.  (If you're wondering, a fair number
  of people do e-mail me to correct mistakes, but most of the time I catch my
  own mistakes.)
* Added huge number of precise technical details, in their own sections.  The
  technical detail is more for myself than anyone else; I don't expect more
  than a handful of people to care about or to be able to fully understand this
  level of detail.
* Now specifically licensed under CC BY-NC 4.0.
* There is some reason to use Tandy graphics and sound, since both EGA and PC
  speaker result in slowdown.
* DOSBox-X is now the preferred emulator for experienced users.
* The configuration file should use DOS/Windows-style line terminators.
* Added DFortae mod mention.  (I'll leave any detailed analysis of it to him.)
* Moved removing copy protection to code hacking; I don't think there'd be many
  people left by now who would need practical help with cracking the game.
* Moved exploit section later on; it was taking too much space up front.
  (Restarting the game was left in place; questions about that are too common.)
* Removed maximum level-up hit point table, because it's too buggy in this game
  to summarize precisely and concisely.
* Sokal Keep: the treasures at locations 15...17 are all once-only.
* Podal Plaza: more detail for the monster tavern free-for-all.
* Podal Plaza: you can't pick the lock to the hidden temple of Ilmater.
* Wealthy Area: crash at (4,15).
* Kobold Caves: corrected penalties.
* Lizardman Keep: corrected penalties.
* Valhingen Graveyard: leaving the graveyard after fighting wights but before
  fighting the spectre at location 22 can cause the latter encounter to fail
  to appear.
* Stojanow Gate: you can't pick the gate lock.
* Valjevo Castle: you can't pick the hedge maze gate locks.
* Valjevo Castle Inner Tower: the endgame graphics differ depending on whether
  you have 1, 2, 3, or 4+ characters in your party.
* Minimum hit point roll for level 1 character rule added.
* Rewrote time discussion, with attention given to the de facto time limit.
* Your characters do get older from means other than Haste after 256 years.
* Experience sharing after battle added.
* Level draining drains the level requiring the most XP, not the most recently
  gained level (the game doesn't remember what the most recent was).
* Detail on duel mechanics added.  Duels are good for low-level elves with
  magic-user levels.
* Removed Bend Bars/Lift Gates from Strength table, as I don't think that ever
  gets used in any Gold Box game; the game engine only supports 2 levels of
  barred door opening difficulty.
* Strength restrictions on maximum level can be circumvented with Strength
  buffs.
* Strength 18 allows a Halfling Fighter to train to level 6.
* Strength 19 does not affect carrying capacity as expected.
* Strength 19...25 unexpected door bashing details added.
* Wisdom does not provide any saving throw bonuses (as the PHB claims).
* Dexterity ranged attack bonuses do not apply to all thrown weapons.
* Dexterity thief skill modifiers are only well behaved from 9...19.
* Dexterity always providing an initiative modifier does not actually adhere to
  D&D 1E rules.
* Constitution is only properly behaved from 4...18.
* Constitution level-up bonus corrected (to what the game incorrectly does).
* Constitution saving throw behavior corrected (to what the game incorrectly
  does).
* Constitution 0 behavior noted: it does not permanently kill the character as
  expected, and grants a larger hit point bonus than Constitution 22.
* Charisma outside 3...25 is not properly handled by the game.
* Dwarves do not get a THAC0 bonus against GOBLIN GUARD (#2), HOBGOBLIN LDR
  (#7), or GRISHNAK (#28).
* Elves (correctly) don't get a to-hit bonus with a Light Crossbow, but they
  also (incorrectly) don't get one with a Fine Composite Long Bow.
* Gnomes do not get a THAC0 bonus against GOBLIN GUARD (#2).
* Fighters may only sweep if they are a PC race.
* Thief abilities are not necessarily immediately recalculated.
* More details on opening locked doors.
* Counting the regular back attack bonuses separately, a backstab is +2 to-hit,
  not +4.
* The to-hit and to-damage bonuses from magical launcher and missiles both
  actually stack.
* Tabletop rules don't describe what a generic magical launcher does, only
  specific ones.  That means that even though Pool of Radiance and Curse of the
  Azure Bonds behave differently from later games, neither can be said to be
  right or wrong.
* No barehanded base damage if attacking a Large creature without a weapon.
* Either party ad hoc combat modifier applies to everyone in the party.
* There is no combat map modifier for defense; that only exists for movement.
* The mechanics for magic resistance are actually implemented, though only
  Tyranthraxus has it.  (I thought it wasn't, because there is no creature
  field for it as in all later Gold Box D&D games.)
* If there is a percent reduction to damage, there is nothing actually assuring
  a minimum of 1 damage.
* While this game does define a roll of 20 as an automatic success for a saving
  throw, that is an optional rule in the tabletop game.
* Added section on the combat arena.
* If trying to flee, line of sight to enemies does not matter if there is any
  enemy at all in line of sight.
* Initiative corrections/additions: (1) higher, not lower, is better; (2)
  surprise inflicts a -6 penalty in the first round; (3) initiative 0 prevents
  action; (4) ties are broken by 1d100 roll; (5) ties after that are always won
  by monsters over characters.
* Spellcasting is allowed in the surprise round.
* Morale checks are based on remaining percent hit points.
* The AI decision to surrender requires minimum Intelligence 6, not 4.
* Notes on how the AI chooses what spell to cast.
* Cure Light Wounds: (1) not significantly more likely to cast depending on
  injuries; (2) notes on how the AI prioritizes targets.
* Hold Person: (1) precise definition of affected creatures added; (2) for
  magic-users, spelled out saving throw penalties.
* Silence causes the AI to never use items.
* Snake Charm: (1) does indeed have no limit whatsoever on area affected; (2)
  does actually scale to caster (current) hit points.  I had this right to
  begin with.
* Spiritual Hammer has a +1 magical bonus, always.
* Animate Dead: (1) can work on "monsters" that are not monstrous humanoids;
  animated creatures (2) are turnable as zombies, regardless of HD; (3) have
  base movement 6; (4) lose all memorized spells; (5) die upon being reduced to
  0 HP; (6) permanently die if level-drained.
* Cause Disease: (1) has a casting time of 100, not 10; (2) lasts for 1d6 * 10
  minutes, not indefinitely (as it is supposed to).
* Dispel Magic: (1) cast by a cleric has a casting time of 4, not 6; (2) does
  not behave as expected against any dispellable effect that does not remember
  its original caster level.
* Prayer only works on allies/enemies within 6 squares of the caster.
* Raise Dead has no chance of failure (the manual is wrong about this).
* Restoration details added.
* Charm Person sets Morale to 100.
* Reduce allows a saving throw vs. spell.  (I'm sure you all care.)
* Shield spell: (1) doesn't work as described in the 1E PHB, it's just AC 3,
  not AC 2 or 3 or 4; (2) does not stop damage from a Wand of Magic Missiles.
* Mirror Image bug detailed mechanism now accounted for.  Also added a simple
  fix proposal.
* Ray of Enfeeblement does not have the additional +2%/level effect.
* Stinking Cloud: (1) the coughing from saving against Stinking Cloud still
  prevents spellcasting; (2) causes the AI to never use items; (3) may work on
  undead in non-IBM versions.
* The Strength spell will not work right on any character having levels in any
  unexpected class (paladin, ranger, monk, or druid).
* Haste and Slow: (1) effects on movement aren't correctly adjusted for when
  viewing a character (the actual movement adjustment works correctly); (2)
  both dispel the other effect *and then* affect number of attacks and
  movement.
* Spell-like magic items added to spell summary.
* Starting gold calculation added.
* Gold value integer overflow bug now mentioned.
* NPC treasure share details corrected (this doesn't work how the game claims).
* Dart range rounding artifact now accounted for.  If you're wondering why the
  "1/2/5" range looks odd ... well, it does, but it's what actually happens.
* Padded armor allows base movement 12, not 9.
* There isn't any functional difference between the different kinds of
  mirrors.
* Holy water does 1d6 damage to vulnerable targets, not 2d4.
* Item entries for Boulder and Large Boulder added.
* Corrected dice for Necklace of Missiles; it doesn't have a flat 6 dice,
  it's 1d3 * 2 + 1 dice.
* More details on selling items (rounding down happens).
* More details on magical treasure XP.
* Complete rewrite of random item generation section, with precise details.
* A random Fine Composite Long Bow is not possible.
* You can in fact get actual Bracers AC 2, but only as a very rare random item.
* You can't get Restoration on a randomly generated clerical scroll.
* A random Cloak of Displacement does actually grant the correct +2 saving
  throw bonus.
* The Cloak of Elvenkind operates anywhere, not just outside.
* Dust of Disappearance targeting and duration depends on whether it is used in
  or out of combat.
* Potion of Giant Strength: (1) does not check the class of its drinker; (2)
  confers the ability to throw boulders.
* The Ring of Invisibility grants invisibility for 1 minute which is renewed
  at the end of every round.
* Wand of Fireball and Wand of Lightning Bolt incorrectly permit a save vs.
  spell (rod/staff/wand is correct).
* Wand of Magic Missiles details and bug added.
* Bestiary entries now include type and effect IDs.
* Monster secondary attacks come before primary attacks.
* VAMPIRE (#23/#105): can be protected against with an equipped mirror or
  Wooden Holy Symbol of Tyr, which prevents the charming gaze.
* TROLL (#31): (1) only begins to regenerate 3 minutes after taking damage; (2)
  can actually be stopped from rising from the dead with fire/acid damage, but
  all damage must be fire/acid.
* STIRGE (#61): immobilizes the victim with its blood draining attack
* AHNKHEG (#65): acid squirt details added.
* TYRANITHRAXUS (#66): (1) dragon fear aura detailed description added; (2)
  actually only has 85% magic resistance (still >100% against a level 6 magical
  attack); (3) has a maximum of 3 breath attacks.
* DRIDER (#69): saving throw is unmodified (incorrectly; the saving throw
  penalty code is partly present).
* GHOUL (#72): paralyzes for 63 rounds.
* GIANT MANTIS (#74): immobilizes only until a 3d4 attack the next round, and
  this only works if the victim has less than Strength 19.
* MUMMY (#114): (1) fear aura allows a +1 saving throw bonus with a party size
  of at least 6; (2) mummy rot detailed description added; (3) vulnerability to
  fire details corrected (including bug).
* Completely reorganized technical data.
* The parser for decompressing DAX files cannot handle run length 127...128.
* Effect 30 isn't nauseated (which suggests helplessness), it's coughing from
  Stinking Cloud.
* Effect 31 is a subset of helpless (there are four effects that are); it
  includes anything not from effects 51...53, with nauseated from Stinking
  Cloud the most common.
* Effect 40 isn't in a Stinking Cloud, it's cast a Stinking Cloud (used to
  track expiration of battlefield clouds).
* Effect 50 is specifically mummy rot heal blocking.
* Effect 57 is mummy rot degeneration.
* Effect 58 is immobile.
* Effect 59 is the troll add regeneration timer.
* Effect 69 does not actually cause a saving throw penalty.
* Effect 74 is currently biting and holding.
* Effect 75 is being blood drained.
* Effect 78 is being healed to positive hit points while unconscious.
* Effect 81 (dragon fear/curse) and 82 (mummy fear) details added.
* Effect 87 is the melee mummy rot attack.
* Effect 90 is the dwarf/halfling Constitution poison saving throw bonus
* Effect 101 is troll adding regeneration timer.
* Effect 102 is troll returning from going down timer.
* Effect 103 is the vampire's assume gaseous form ability.
* Effect 105 is 50% magic resistance.  (Not ever used.)
* Effect 106 is 85% magic resistance.  (Not 100%.)
* Effect 126 isn't immunity to gaze attacks, it's gaze attack susceptibility to
  being prevented.
* The remaining three unknown effects (60, 63, 92) will likely remain unknown,
  unless we ever get Pool of Radiance design documents or source code.
* Added file manifest.
* Overview of Gold Box Virtual Machine mechanics added.
* ECL variable type 3 (pointer to uint16) does appear in this game.
* ECL opcode 1E CHECKPARTY description expanded and corrected.
* ECL opcode 21 LOAD FILES description added.
* ECL opcodes 22 PARTY SURPRISE and 23 SURPRISE set and apply the wrong
  modifiers for surprise.
* ECL opcode 28 ROB has issues.  Added hints for dealing with in gameplay.
* ECL opcode 29 ENCOUNTER MENU also allows the party to catch fleeing monsters.
* ECL opcode 2D CALL now has a complete list of recognized operands.
* ECL opcode 2E DAMAGE corrected.
* ECL opcode 30 OR also performs a comparison.
* ECL opcode 34 ECL CLOCK is coded incorrectly (though it turns out not to
  matter because it's never used).
* ECL opcode 36 ADD NPC makes the added NPC friendly, except for LEVEL 6 MU
  (#24).
* ECL opcode 37 LOAD PIECES description added.
* ECL opcode 3B SPELL is coded and called, but incorrectly and is a no-op.
* Removed GBVM addresses that the game never uses or recognizes.  (I'd included
  several that only other Gold Box games use.)
* GBVM $49E5 is the elusive anti-magic flag.
* GBVM $6DC7 is set to 128 for a total party kill.  (It's implemented but not
  ever actually used here; it does actually get used in the sequel.)
* GBVM $6E71 only modifies THAC0.
* STING cheat now fully detailed.
* Overview of save file format added.
* Greatly expanded code overview section.  (The majority of additions in this
  revision are here.)
* Expanded code hacking section.
* Added references to Deities & Demigods.
* Added QuestBusters references.

v1.50 (2021-11-07)
* Changed quotes to be more like how the game works (single for in-game
  quotations, double for game descriptive text).
* Changed location numbering to more closely match both the game's internal
  numbering and Gold Box Companion.
* Added more game text.
* Added more notes on the game not properly registering fleeing.
* The game's Constitution hit point table actually does not go above 20.  (All
  subsequent games have the table go up to 25.)  Anything above that causes
  array-out-of-bounds shenanigans.
* Slow does not either stack cumulatively with itself, or affect allies, unlike
  the tabletop version.
* A Wand of Paralyzation actually requires a save vs. paralyzation, not the
  correct rod/staff/wand.
* Added link to Null Null's single-character Let's Play.
* Added link to CRPG Addict.
* Added list of ECL scripts and maps.
* Moved spell table format to data structure section.
* Executable structure format rearranged.
* The Acolyte NPC can be modified with strange results.
* Some script flag additions and corrections.
* Sokal Keep: the three words on the parchment do have real-life meanings.
* Kuto's Well: random monsters only sometimes attack with high morale.
* Kuto's Well Catacombs: Norris the Gray's treasure may disappear if you fail
  to claim it promptly.
* Mendor's Library: the maximum number of green slime encounters is 7, not 8.
* Cadorna Textiile House: there are different destinations if you flee from
  hobgoblins.
* Wilderness: consoliated advice on the most dangerous monsters.
* Yarash's Pyramid Level 1: a couple of the maze random encounters have
  surprise roll bonuses.
* Yarash's Pyramid Level 2 and 3: corrected teleporter details.
* Yarash's Pyramid Level 3: added mechanics for destroying the machinery.
* Valjevo Castle Inner Tower: noted scripting bug that never fully marks
  Tyranthraxus as defeated.

v1.40 (2020-11-22)
* Overhauled location numbers to mostly match those actually used by the game,
  and by Gold Box Companion and Gold Box Explorer.  (I hadn't known about any
  of these before I wrote the original version.)  There are still places where
  I don't use the internal numbering, to keep things simpler.  This all makes
  this version incompatible with all previous versions, if going into that
  level of detail.
* Split each map section into an overview section and a details section.
* Added more game text.
* Added coordinates to map annotations, if not ambiguous.
* Added full section on Wilderness Lairs.
* Added formalized list of bugs.  (This is not comprehensive by any means.)
* Added detail on how to successfully backstab.
* Added yet another reason not to use NPCs: they make random combats larger.
* Added strategy for using Animate Dead.
* You can in fact fight Ogre Leaders, in the Wealthy Area or Temple of Bane.
  It requires a high Party Strength to have any chance of happening (at least
  66) and is unlikely even then.
* Snake Charm and Animate Dead scale to caster level, not caster hit points.
* Dust of Disappearance will work even against enemies that see invisible, and
  is supposed to.
* Magical armor never actually provides a saving throw bonus.
* Rings of Protection rarely actually provide a saving throw bonus.  The only
  one I know of where that actually works is the one carried by the THEURGIST
  (#110).  (Another case where I was led astray by that working more reliably
  in the sequels.)
* Added more executable offsets: classes allowed by race, statistics by class.
* Civilized Area: more detail on available NPCs.
* Civilized Area: clarified that you must explicitly receive the commission for
  Podal Plaza to both perform and be rewarded for it.
* Slums: the script doesn't check if you ran away before crediting you with
  victory against a set combat.
* Slums: add description of bug that allows you to clear the area without
  fighting any set combats.
* Sokal Keep: searching for the diary only works reliably if you were told
  about it by the spectral defenders.
* Mendor's Library: more detail, including mechanics for green slime in the
  garden.
* Podal Plaza: parley mechanics expanded and corrected.
* Cadorna Textile House: you can't ever get all the random encounters without
  defeating the ogre.
* Cadorna Textile House: added exploit which allows you to open the Cadorna
  family treasure repeatedly.
* Wealthy Area: random encounter probability corrected.
* Wealthy Area: added detail on how random encounters are constructed and
  resolved.
* Wealthy Area: the four-orc patrol counts as encountered as long as you do
  anything other than fight them.
* Nomad Camp: the Shaman's Bracers AC 2 is actually AC 4.
* Yarash's Pyramid: now split into two separate sections, both for clarity and
  because that's how the game handles it internally.
* Buccaneer Base: resting is disturbed by the next random encounter, if active.
* Valhingen Graveyard: the two-handed sword vs. undead isn't actually a good
  choice here.
* Stojanow Gate: more detail and accuracy.

v1.32 (2020-06-07)
* Added effect of transfer to Hillsfar.

v1.31 (2020-05-19)
* Added this version history (retroactive to all previous versions).
* Moved references/acknowledgments to the end; it was taking up too much space
  at the beginning.
* Added note that the best way to reach me is by e-mail.  (I belatedly realized
  that there were lots of comments in places I wasn't checking regularly.)
* Charm Person works on the magic-user in Valhingen Graveyard to convert him to
  a fully friendly NPC.

v1.30 (2020-05-10)
* I overhauled the script that generates monster statistics.  There is more
  information, and most of the mistakes from the days when I added statistics
  by hand should be gone.  (The AI I wrote was a little bit too intelligent --
  the real Pool of Radiance AI favors studded leather armor over Bracers AC 6
  for ... reasons.)
* There is more accurate information on how much XP money and items give.
  That's also now part of the monster statistic generator script.
* All placed magically charged treasure now mentions how many charges it starts
  with.
* The spellcasting delay adjustment is actually division by 3, not 2.  That
  makes spellcasting significantly more powerful than it is in the tabletop
  game.  (So even though Stinking Cloud has a tabletop game delay of 2 ... that
  gets divided by 3, then rounded down to 0, so it actually has no delay in the
  Gold Box games.)
* There are now instructions on how to modify spellcasting delay, in case you
  want something closer to the actual tabletop rules.  (It'll significantly
  alter game balance, of course.)
* Added how AI spellcasting priority works.
* With both spellcasting delay and AI priority not obvious and not in the
  manual, added a table.
* Clarification of area of effect spells: it wasn't clear before what "diameter
  3/5/7" meant.
* You can raise the dead by casting a targeted spell like Cause Light Wounds or
  Burning Hands in combat. (This bug is gone starting in Secret of the Silver
  Blades.)
* More detail on how Animate Dead works.
* Correction for the Sleep spell.  (Yet again.  I think I really do have it
  right this time.)
* The Stinking Cloud AC penalty is larger than I thought.
* Mirror Image is not as effective as it should be, or as in later games. (Also
  fixed in Secret of the Silver Blades.)
* Haste and Slow do not actually affect initiative.
* Strength: for Open Doors, easy vs. hard is in fact implemented, so I put that
  in the table.
* Dexterity: actual adjustments to thief skills.  There are a couple wrong
  numbers in the game's table.
* For avoiding attacks, being third-from-the-bottom is better than being #5 in
  the order.
* A "small/medium" target for a weapon is only so if it BOTH has that size in
  the game files AND has a 1-tile icon.
* For some reason, I thought you couldn't backstab Large opponents in the 1st
  Edition tabletop game.  There's no such rule.  (There *is* a rule much like
  that in 2nd Edition.)
* Added the actual THAC0 table used by the game.  It's a hybrid of 1st/2nd
  edition rules (as you might expect), and it also changes by specific Gold Box
  game.
* More information on binary format.
* Added Computer Gaming World references.
* Added Acknowledgments.

v1.22 (2019-07-11)
* Fixed up a lot of technical details I had added.

v1.21 (2019-07-10)
* "Porphyrys Cadorna" actually had a "y" in it I missed.
* Mention of the not-implemented tabletop rule about firing missiles into a
  melee.

v1.20 (2019-07-07)
* THAC0 in the bestiary corrected for how the game actually calculates it.
* Added Morale mechanic section, and specific monster morale in most cases.
* Added Party Strength calculation.
* Added distance that monsters start at, and what Distance 1 and 2 mean.
* Added more detail on how random encounters are generated and resolved.
* Added more detail everywhere on how safe resting is.
* Added detail on where "F"leeing will move the party.
* Added detail on damage the party can take outside of combat.
* Added temple spell casting section.
* Added surprise rules (which only Pool of Radiance fully supports).
* Added detail on how the game handles time.
* Added range for throwable melee weapons.
* Added many more technical notes.
* Correction for the Sleep spell.  (Again.)
* Thrown weapons do not get a Strength bonus.  (That's a 2nd Edition rule.)
* Valhingen Graveyard commission moved to its own subsection, because the game
  special-cases it in so many ways, and added a lot more detail on it.
* Warning about items randomly disappearing -- it's not actually a bug as
  people sometimes think.  You probably got pickpocketed in Phlan's taverns.
* It does not generally actually help to lower statistics for the sake of
  gaming Party Strength.
* Added note that the game doesn't bother to implement the proper rules about
  recovering from being knocked unconscious.
* Civilized Area: much more information about how visiting a tavern works.
  (That was way back when I was first starting to write this ...)
* Civilized Area: more information about hirable NPCs.
* Civilized Area: added triggers for the proclamations changing.
* Slums: you must deal with Ohlo one way or the other to clear the block.
* Podal Plaza: in version 1.3, if you do not screw up the mission, the block
  should always be clearable.
* Kovel Mansion: even more precision on how number of thief kills works.
* Kobold Caves: more details on traps.
* Lizardman Keep: more precision on how number of lizard man kills works.
* Valhingen Graveyard: more detail on how monsters can regenerate.
* Valhingen Graveyard: discovered conditions under which sanctification works.
* Stojanow Gate: added how the roll for bluffing the bugbears works.
* Valjevo Castle: more detail on how talking with the washerwomen works.
* Extended warning about hit point overflow if you are hacking.
* Monsters #9 and #82 are thought not to actually appear anywhere.

v1.10 (2019-04-07)
* Added advice for starting stats rolled normally.
* Added section on restarting the game.  (It gets asked fairly often.)
* Added section on transferring characters to Curse.  (Asked even more often.)
* Added experience value table for gold/platinum/gems/jewelry.
* Added first version of the Flag List.
* Correction for the Sleep spell.
* Civilized Area: two ways to learn that Porphyrys Cadorna is a traitor.
* Kovel Mansion: more precision on how number of thief kills works.
* Valhingen Graveyard: Skeleton/Zombie/Wight zones now marked (because that
  last one is dangerous).
* Moved my primary e-mail address after decades.
* Gold Box Companion moved to a new Web page.

v1.02 (2016-10-05)
* Corrected error on statistics transferring to Curse of the Azure Bonds.
* Added how to crack version 1.3.
* Added link to FRUA fan remake.

v1.01 (2016-10-04)
* More notes on the Necklace of Missiles.

v1.00 (2016-10-03)
* Original release.

Known unfixed/untested issues:
* Fix multidimensional array descriptions so they are consistently row-major;
  my writing "X*Y" does not reliably reflect which dimension actually comes
  first.
* GRISHNAK (#28) or MACE (#33) AI suspected to attempt to cast Cure Light
  Wounds on unconscious characters; this is expected to be rare.
* Effect 47, already buggy, is suspected to not work at all.

No plans to fix:
* Scripted quotes are not replicated precisely.  This is mostly becuase I THINK
  READING TEXT LIKE THIS IS VERY ANNOYING.  The same goes for any non-diegetic
  misspelling (which I tend to correct).
* I'm not going to change my writing style, which may raise red flags as
  LLM-generated (e.g., I use em dashes far more often than most actual people).
  I don't plan to ever use an LLM to write this.  LLM-generated writing tends
  to use better writing as their sampling source, and it takes extra effort to
  avoid red flags by deliberately playing dumb.

SECTION 15.3 - Acknowledgments
------------------------------

Acknowledgments (not comprehensive):
  * Joonas Hirvonen (author of Gold Box Companion)
  * Simeon Pilgrim (author of the modernized Curse of the Azure Bonds)
  * CondorMan (discoverer of many bugs only present in earlier games)
  * Alpha3KV (for information on transferring characters)

SECTION 15.4 - Things I Want
----------------------------

Things I'd like (please e-mail me if you have any of the below):

(1) a complete, original physical copy of the IBM version 1.0 or 1.1
(2) what does "ECL" stand for?  (Nope, no idea!)
